lessons.title: What Economics Actually Is
lessons.content:
Economics is the study of how people, businesses, and governments make decisions when resources are limited. That limitation is the starting point for everything else in this subject.

Resources are scarce. There is only so much land, time, money, labor, and raw material available at any moment. Because of this scarcity, every choice involves a tradeoff. When you spend an hour studying, you are not spending that hour sleeping or working. When a government spends money on roads, that money is not being spent on schools. Every decision to do one thing is simultaneously a decision not to do something else.

Economics is split into two main branches. Microeconomics focuses on the decisions of individuals, households, and firms. It asks questions like: why does the price of coffee rise when a frost hits Brazil? How do firms decide how much to produce? How do wages get determined?

Macroeconomics zooms out and looks at entire economies. It asks: why does a country's unemployment rate rise during a recession? What causes inflation? How do interest rates affect economic growth?

Both branches use models. A model is a simplified representation of reality that isolates specific relationships and helps us think clearly. No model captures everything, and that is by design. The goal is clarity, not completeness.

examples.label: Scarcity in Practice
examples.code:
A city has a budget of $10 million for public services.

Option A: Build a new hospital wing.
Option B: Repair 50 miles of road.
Option C: Hire 200 new teachers.

Choosing one means giving up the others.
This is scarcity forcing a tradeoff.
Economics studies how and why that choice gets made.

lessons.title: Opportunity Cost
lessons.content:
The opportunity cost of a choice is the value of the next best alternative you gave up by making that choice. It is not just what you spent. It is what you could have had instead.

If you have $1000 and you spend it on a vacation, the opportunity cost is not just $1000. It is whatever that $1000 would have produced if you had used it differently. If the next best option was investing it at 8% interest, the opportunity cost is $1080 next year.

Opportunity cost applies to time as well. A doctor who decides to mow their own lawn instead of hiring someone has a very high opportunity cost. The hour they spent mowing is an hour they could have spent seeing patients and earning several hundred dollars.

This is why economists say there is no such thing as a free lunch. Even if something has no monetary price, it consumes time, attention, or space that could have gone to something else. The cost is always there. It just is not always obvious.

Understanding opportunity cost is what separates economic thinking from surface-level thinking. Most people count what they spend. Economists also count what they gave up.

examples.label: The College Decision
examples.code:
You decide to go to college for 4 years.

Direct costs: $120,000 in tuition and fees.

Opportunity cost: The salary you could have earned
working full-time for 4 years. If that is $35,000/year,
the opportunity cost is $140,000.

Total economic cost of college:
$120,000 + $140,000 = $260,000

Not $120,000. That is why the economic return
on a degree needs to be high to justify the choice.

lessons.title: The Production Possibilities Frontier
lessons.content:
The Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF) is a model that shows the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce when all resources are fully and efficiently used.

Points on the curve represent efficient production. Points inside the curve represent inefficiency. Points outside the curve are currently impossible with available resources and technology.

The shape of the PPF matters. A straight PPF means constant opportunity cost: giving up one unit of good A always produces the same number of units of good B. A bowed-out PPF means increasing opportunity cost: as you produce more of one good, each additional unit costs you more of the other good. This happens because resources are not equally suited to producing all goods.

The PPF shifts outward when an economy grows. This happens through more resources, better technology, or improved productivity. A technological advance in one sector shifts only part of the curve.

The PPF also illustrates the concept of comparative advantage. Even if one country or person can produce everything more efficiently than another, both can still gain from specialization and trade.

examples.label: Reading the PPF
examples.code:
An economy produces only wheat and steel.

At point A: 100 wheat, 0 steel (all resources on wheat)
At point B: 80 wheat, 20 steel
At point C: 50 wheat, 40 steel (balanced production)
At point D: 0 wheat, 50 steel (all resources on steel)

All points A through D are on the frontier: efficient.
A point producing 40 wheat and 10 steel is inside
the frontier: inefficient, resources are being wasted.
A point of 100 wheat and 50 steel is outside: impossible now.

lessons.title: Types of Economic Systems
lessons.content:
Every society must answer three fundamental economic questions. What should be produced? How should it be produced? For whom should it be produced? The way a society answers these questions defines its economic system.

A market economy answers these questions through prices and voluntary exchange. Buyers and sellers interact in markets, and prices emerge from those interactions. Prices carry information and coordinate decisions without any central authority directing them. The United States, Canada, and most of Western Europe operate as market economies.

A command economy answers these questions through central planning. A government authority decides what gets produced, in what quantities, and who receives it. The Soviet Union operated this way. Command economies can mobilize resources quickly for specific goals but struggle with the information problem: no central planner has access to all the local knowledge that markets aggregate through prices.

A mixed economy combines elements of both. Most real economies today are mixed. Governments regulate certain industries, provide public goods, and redistribute income, while markets handle most day-to-day production and exchange.

A traditional economy answers these questions based on custom and historical practice. Production follows patterns established by prior generations. These exist in isolated communities and declining numbers.

examples.label: Three Questions Across Systems
examples.code:
Question: What to produce?
Market: Whatever buyers are willing to pay for.
Command: Whatever the planning authority decides.
Traditional: Whatever has always been produced.

Question: How to produce it?
Market: Whatever method is most profitable.
Command: Whatever method the plan specifies.
Traditional: Whatever method ancestors used.

Question: For whom?
Market: Those who can afford it.
Command: Whoever the authority allocates to.
Traditional: Distributed by custom and role.

lessons.title: Thinking at the Margin
lessons.content:
Economists think in terms of marginal changes. A marginal change is a small, incremental adjustment to a plan or decision. The question is never should you do this or not. The question is should you do a little more or a little less of this.

Marginal benefit is the additional benefit gained from one more unit of something. Marginal cost is the additional cost incurred by producing or consuming one more unit.

The core principle: a rational decision-maker continues an activity as long as the marginal benefit exceeds the marginal cost. They stop when marginal cost exceeds marginal benefit. The optimal point is where marginal benefit equals marginal cost.

This applies everywhere. How many hours should you study? Keep studying as long as the benefit of one more hour exceeds the cost. How much should a factory produce? Keep producing as long as the revenue from one more unit exceeds the cost of producing it.

Sunk costs are costs that have already been incurred and cannot be recovered. Economic thinking says sunk costs should be ignored in current decisions. What you spent in the past does not change the marginal costs and benefits you face right now. People who keep watching a bad movie because they paid for it are committing the sunk cost fallacy.

examples.label: Marginal Decision: How Much to Produce
examples.code:
Unit 1: Revenue $50, Cost $20. Net benefit: +$30. Produce.
Unit 2: Revenue $45, Cost $25. Net benefit: +$20. Produce.
Unit 3: Revenue $40, Cost $35. Net benefit: +$5. Produce.
Unit 4: Revenue $35, Cost $40. Net benefit: -$5. Stop.

The rational firm stops at unit 3.
Unit 4 costs more than it earns.
This is marginal analysis.

questions.q: What is scarcity in economics?
options: When prices are very high.
When resources are limited relative to unlimited human wants, forcing tradeoffs.
When a country is poor.
When a product is rare and collectible.
questions.explanation: Scarcity is the fundamental condition that makes economics necessary. Because resources are finite and wants are not, every choice involves giving something up. Scarcity applies to rich countries and poor ones alike.

questions.q: What is the opportunity cost of a decision?
options: The price paid for a good or service.
The value of the best alternative that was given up by making that choice.
The total cost of all alternatives.
The regret a person feels after choosing.
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is not just what you spent. It is the value of the next best thing you could have done instead. It captures the true cost of any choice.

questions.q: What does a point inside the Production Possibilities Frontier represent?
options: The most efficient allocation possible.
A combination of output that is currently impossible.
Inefficiency: resources are not being fully or properly used.
A situation where the economy is growing.
questions.explanation: Points inside the PPF represent underperformance. The economy is producing less than it is capable of with its available resources. Points on the frontier are efficient. Points outside are currently unattainable.

questions.q: What is the marginal cost?
options: The total cost of producing all units.
The average cost per unit.
The additional cost of producing one more unit.
The cost of the first unit produced.
questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the cost added by producing exactly one additional unit. It is not the average and not the total. Marginal analysis uses this figure to determine optimal output decisions.

questions.q: What is the sunk cost fallacy?
options: Investing too much in a profitable venture.
Letting past costs that cannot be recovered influence current decisions, when they should be ignored.
Underestimating the cost of future projects.
Spending money on something with no resale value.
questions.explanation: Sunk costs are gone regardless of what you decide now. Rational economic decisions are based on current marginal costs and benefits. Continuing a bad project because you already invested in it is the sunk cost fallacy.

questions.q: What makes an economy a mixed economy?
options: It produces both goods and services.
It combines market mechanisms with government intervention and public provision.
It trades with both rich and poor countries.
It has both large and small firms.
questions.explanation: A mixed economy uses markets for most production and exchange decisions while also involving government through regulation, public goods, and redistribution. Most modern economies are mixed.

lessons.title: Protocol: Calculate Opportunity Cost
lessons.prompt:
You have 3 hours free on Saturday. You can use them to:

Option A: Work a part-time job at $20/hour = $60
Option B: Study for an exam
Option C: Watch movies

You choose to study. What is the opportunity cost of studying?

Write only the dollar amount of the opportunity cost.

validation.message: error: the opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative given up. you chose to study instead of working, so the opportunity cost is the $60 you could have earned.

lessons.title: Demand
lessons.content:
Demand is the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that buyers are willing and able to purchase, holding all other factors constant. The key phrase is willing and able. Wanting something is not demand. Having the willingness and the purchasing power together is demand.

The Law of Demand states that as price increases, quantity demanded decreases, and vice versa. This is an inverse relationship. The reason is straightforward: when something costs more, some buyers can no longer afford it, and others find substitutes.

A demand curve graphs this relationship with price on the vertical axis and quantity on the horizontal axis. It typically slopes downward from left to right.

It is critical to distinguish between a change in quantity demanded and a change in demand. A change in quantity demanded is a movement along the existing demand curve caused by a price change. A change in demand is a shift of the entire curve caused by something other than price.

Things that shift the demand curve include: income changes, prices of related goods (substitutes or complements), consumer tastes and preferences, expectations about future prices, and the number of buyers in the market.

Substitutes are goods that can replace each other. If the price of butter rises, demand for margarine increases. Complements are goods used together. If the price of printers rises, demand for ink cartridges falls.

examples.label: Shift vs Movement
examples.code:
Movement along the curve:
The price of coffee rises from $3 to $4.
Consumers buy fewer cups per week.
This is a change in quantity demanded, not demand.

Shift of the curve:
A study is published showing coffee reduces health risks.
At every price, consumers want more coffee than before.
The entire demand curve shifts right.
This is a change in demand.

lessons.title: Supply
lessons.content:
Supply is the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that producers are willing and able to sell, holding all other factors constant. The Law of Supply states that as price increases, quantity supplied increases. This is a positive relationship. Higher prices make it more profitable to produce and attract more sellers into the market.

A supply curve slopes upward from left to right.

As with demand, distinguish between a change in quantity supplied and a change in supply. A price change moves you along the supply curve. A non-price factor shifts the entire curve.

Things that shift the supply curve include: input costs (wages, raw materials), technology improvements, number of sellers, government taxes and subsidies, and expectations about future prices.

If wages rise, it costs more to produce, so supply decreases and the curve shifts left. If a new technology makes production cheaper, supply increases and the curve shifts right.

A tax on a product increases the cost of selling it, which reduces supply. A subsidy reduces the cost of selling, which increases supply.

examples.label: What Shifts Supply
examples.code:
Supply increases (curve shifts right):
- New technology cuts production costs
- Price of a key input falls
- Government provides a production subsidy
- More firms enter the market

Supply decreases (curve shifts left):
- Cost of labor rises
- Raw material becomes scarce
- Government imposes a new production tax
- Firms exit the market

lessons.title: Market Equilibrium
lessons.content:
Market equilibrium is the price at which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded. At this price, the market clears: every buyer who wants the good at that price can find a seller, and every seller who wants to sell at that price can find a buyer.

When price is above equilibrium, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded. This creates a surplus. Sellers are producing more than buyers want at that price. The surplus puts downward pressure on price, moving the market back toward equilibrium.

When price is below equilibrium, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. This creates a shortage. Buyers want more than sellers are producing at that price. The shortage puts upward pressure on price, moving the market back toward equilibrium.

This self-correcting process is what economists mean when they say markets tend toward equilibrium. Price acts as a signal: too high means produce less or lower the price; too low means produce more or raise the price.

When supply or demand shifts, a new equilibrium is established. If demand increases while supply stays constant, the equilibrium price rises and equilibrium quantity rises. If supply decreases while demand stays constant, the equilibrium price rises and equilibrium quantity falls.

examples.label: Shift in Demand: Finding New Equilibrium
examples.code:
Original equilibrium: Price = $10, Quantity = 500 units

Event: A new study shows this product has major health benefits.
Demand increases: the demand curve shifts right.

At the old price of $10: quantity demanded is now 700
but quantity supplied is still 500. Shortage of 200 units.

Price rises. At $13, quantity demanded falls back toward 580
and quantity supplied rises to 580.

New equilibrium: Price = $13, Quantity = 580 units.

lessons.title: Elasticity
lessons.content:
Elasticity measures how responsive quantity is to a change in price or another variable. It tells us not just that demand falls when price rises, but how much it falls.

Price elasticity of demand measures how much quantity demanded changes in response to a price change. The formula is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.

If the result is greater than 1, demand is elastic: quantity responds strongly to price changes. Consumers are sensitive to price. Goods with many substitutes tend to be elastic.

If the result is less than 1, demand is inelastic: quantity responds weakly to price changes. Consumers buy roughly the same amount regardless of price changes. Necessities with few substitutes, like insulin for diabetics, tend to be inelastic.

If the result equals 1, demand is unit elastic.

Elasticity matters for revenue. If demand is elastic, raising price decreases total revenue because quantity falls by a larger percentage than price rose. If demand is inelastic, raising price increases total revenue because quantity falls by a smaller percentage than price rose.

Income elasticity of demand measures how quantity demanded responds to changes in consumer income. Normal goods have positive income elasticity: demand rises with income. Inferior goods have negative income elasticity: demand falls as income rises.

examples.label: Calculating Elasticity
examples.code:
Price rises from $10 to $12 (20% increase).
Quantity demanded falls from 100 to 80 (20% decrease).

Elasticity = 20% / 20% = 1.0 (unit elastic)

Price rises from $10 to $12 (20% increase).
Quantity demanded falls from 100 to 60 (40% decrease).

Elasticity = 40% / 20% = 2.0 (elastic)
Consumers are very responsive to the price change.

Price rises from $10 to $12 (20% increase).
Quantity demanded falls from 100 to 95 (5% decrease).

Elasticity = 5% / 20% = 0.25 (inelastic)
Consumers barely change behavior when price rises.

lessons.title: Price Controls
lessons.content:
Sometimes governments intervene in markets by setting prices above or below the equilibrium price. These are called price controls.

A price ceiling is a legal maximum price. The government says prices cannot go above this level. For a price ceiling to be binding (to have any effect), it must be set below the equilibrium price. If you set a maximum price of $100 when the market price is $60, the ceiling does nothing.

A binding price ceiling creates a shortage. At the artificially low price, consumers want more than producers are willing to supply. The most famous example is rent control. When rents are capped below market equilibrium, more people want apartments than landlords are willing to rent out at that price.

A price floor is a legal minimum price. For a floor to be binding, it must be set above the equilibrium price. A binding price floor creates a surplus. At the artificially high price, producers supply more than consumers want to buy.

The minimum wage is a price floor in the labor market. When the minimum wage is set above the market equilibrium wage for low-skill labor, employers hire fewer workers than they would at the lower wage, potentially creating unemployment.

Both types of price controls create inefficiencies and lead to non-price rationing mechanisms: waiting lines, connections, discrimination, and black markets.

examples.label: Price Ceiling: Rent Control
examples.code:
Market equilibrium rent: $2,000/month
Government sets rent ceiling: $1,400/month

At $1,400: 10,000 apartments demanded, 6,000 supplied.
Shortage: 4,000 apartments.

Consequences:
- Long waiting lists
- Landlords select tenants using non-price criteria
- Landlords reduce maintenance (less incentive to improve)
- Fewer new apartments built
- Black market subletting at above-ceiling prices

questions.q: The Law of Demand states that as price increases, quantity demanded:
options: Increases
Stays the same
Decreases
First increases then decreases
questions.explanation: The Law of Demand describes an inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded. When price rises, some buyers exit the market or switch to substitutes. Quantity demanded falls.

questions.q: What causes a shift in the demand curve rather than a movement along it?
options: A change in the price of the good itself.
A change in consumer income, preferences, or the price of related goods.
A change in production technology.
A change in the number of firms in the market.
questions.explanation: Only a price change moves you along a fixed demand curve. Everything else (income, substitutes, complements, expectations, number of buyers) shifts the entire curve to a new position.

questions.q: When price is above equilibrium, what happens in the market?
options: A shortage develops and prices rise further.
A surplus develops and downward pressure on price pushes it back toward equilibrium.
The market splits into two separate markets.
Supply decreases to match the higher price.
questions.explanation: Above equilibrium, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded. Unsold inventory builds up. Sellers lower prices to clear the surplus. This moves the market back toward the equilibrium price.

questions.q: Demand for a good is elastic. The seller raises the price. What happens to total revenue?
options: Total revenue rises because the higher price offsets the drop in sales.
Total revenue falls because quantity drops by a larger percentage than price rose.
Total revenue stays the same because elasticity equals 1.
Total revenue rises because elastic demand means fewer substitutes.
questions.explanation: With elastic demand, consumers are sensitive to price. When price rises, quantity falls by proportionally more, so total revenue (price times quantity) declines. Sellers of elastic goods should not raise prices to increase revenue.

questions.q: A binding price ceiling is set:
options: Above the equilibrium price.
Exactly at the equilibrium price.
Below the equilibrium price.
At whatever price the government chooses.
questions.explanation: A price ceiling must be below the market equilibrium price to have any effect. If it is set above equilibrium, the market price is already below the ceiling and the law changes nothing. A binding ceiling creates a shortage.

questions.q: Which of these goods is most likely to have inelastic demand?
options: Luxury handbags
Insulin for diabetics
Restaurant meals
Streaming subscriptions
questions.explanation: Inelastic goods are necessities with few or no substitutes. Insulin is a life-sustaining medication for diabetics with no alternative. Quantity demanded does not fall much even when price rises significantly.

questions.q: The price of steel rises sharply. What happens to the supply of cars, which require steel to produce?
options: Supply of cars increases because steel is now more valuable.
Supply of cars is unaffected because they are different markets.
Supply of cars decreases because input costs have risen.
Demand for cars increases to compensate.
questions.explanation: Steel is an input in car production. When input costs rise, it becomes more expensive to produce each car. This reduces the willingness of car manufacturers to supply at every price, shifting the supply curve left.

lessons.title: Protocol: Predict the New Equilibrium
lessons.prompt:
The market for electric vehicles is at equilibrium. Then two things happen simultaneously:

1. The government offers buyers a $5,000 subsidy to purchase electric vehicles.
2. A new battery technology cuts production costs by 40%.

What direction does equilibrium price move: up, down, or uncertain? Write one word.

validation.message: error: demand increases (subsidy to buyers) and supply increases (lower production costs). both shifts increase equilibrium quantity, but the price effect depends on which shift is larger. price could go up, down, or stay the same. the correct answer is uncertain.

lessons.title: Utility and Consumer Choice
lessons.content:
Utility is the satisfaction or benefit a consumer receives from consuming a good or service. It is a way of measuring how much a consumer values something, even though utility cannot be directly observed or measured in standard units.

Total utility is the overall satisfaction from consuming a given quantity of a good. Marginal utility is the additional satisfaction from consuming one more unit.

The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility states that as you consume more of a good, the marginal utility of each additional unit decreases. The first slice of pizza tastes great. The second is still good. By the fifth slice, you are barely enjoying it. By the seventh, you regret it.

Consumers maximize utility by allocating their budget so that the marginal utility per dollar spent is equal across all goods they consume. This is called the utility-maximizing condition. If the last dollar spent on coffee gives you more utility than the last dollar spent on tea, you should buy more coffee and less tea until those marginal utilities equalize.

Consumer surplus is the difference between the maximum price a consumer is willing to pay for a good and the actual price they pay. If you would pay $50 for a concert ticket and the ticket costs $30, your consumer surplus is $20. Markets create consumer surplus, which represents value that buyers receive beyond what they pay.

examples.label: Diminishing Marginal Utility
examples.code:
Slices of pizza consumed and marginal utility:

Slice 1: Marginal utility = 20
Slice 2: Marginal utility = 15
Slice 3: Marginal utility = 10
Slice 4: Marginal utility = 4
Slice 5: Marginal utility = 0
Slice 6: Marginal utility = -5 (you feel sick)

Total utility rises until slice 5 then falls.
Marginal utility falls with each additional unit.
You stop eating when marginal utility hits zero
or when it falls below the marginal cost of eating more.

lessons.title: Budget Constraints and Choices
lessons.content:
A consumer's budget constraint shows all combinations of goods they can afford with a given income at given prices. It is a real limitation that shapes every consumer decision.

If you have $100 and apples cost $2 and oranges cost $5, you can buy 50 apples and no oranges, or 20 oranges and no apples, or any combination in between that does not exceed $100.

When income increases, the budget constraint shifts outward: the consumer can afford more of everything. When income decreases, it shifts inward.

When the price of one good changes, the budget constraint rotates. If apples get cheaper, you can now buy more apples with the same budget, so the apple end of the constraint extends outward.

Rational consumers choose the combination of goods that gives them the highest utility subject to their budget constraint. This is where consumer theory connects to demand: when price falls, the consumer can reach a higher level of satisfaction, which generally leads them to buy more of the good.

The substitution effect and income effect both explain why quantity demanded increases when price falls. The substitution effect is the shift toward the now-cheaper good. The income effect is the increase in real purchasing power the consumer experiences when a good they buy gets cheaper.

examples.label: Substitution and Income Effects
examples.code:
You spend $40/month on gas.
The price of gas falls by half.

Substitution effect:
Gas is now cheaper relative to other ways of traveling.
You drive more instead of taking the bus.

Income effect:
You now spend only $20/month on the same gas.
You have $20 extra purchasing power.
You use it to buy other goods OR drive even more.

Both effects push quantity demanded of gas upward.
This is why the demand curve slopes downward.

lessons.title: Behavioral Economics
lessons.content:
Standard economic theory assumes that consumers are rational: they have consistent preferences, process information accurately, and always make decisions that maximize their utility. Behavioral economics challenges this assumption by studying how people actually make decisions, which often deviates from the rational model.

Loss aversion is one of the most robust findings in behavioral economics. People feel the pain of a loss roughly twice as strongly as they feel the pleasure of an equivalent gain. Losing $100 feels worse than gaining $100 feels good. This means people take fewer risks than a rational model would predict.

Anchoring occurs when people rely too heavily on the first piece of information they see. If a jacket is listed as originally $300 but now $150, consumers evaluate it differently than if it were simply priced at $150 from the start, even though the actual cost is identical.

Present bias means people disproportionately prefer immediate rewards over future ones. Most people say they would choose $100 today over $110 in a week, even though waiting one week for a 10% return would be an extraordinary investment by any standard.

Nudge theory suggests that the way choices are presented, not just the choices themselves, significantly affects decisions. Setting healthy food as the default option in a cafeteria increases healthy eating without removing any choices. This has applications in public policy, retirement savings, and public health.

examples.label: Loss Aversion in Practice
examples.code:
A rational agent offered a coin flip:
Heads: win $110
Tails: lose $100

Expected value: (+$110 + -$100) / 2 = +$5
A purely rational person should take this bet.

Most real people refuse it.
The potential loss of $100 looms larger psychologically
than the potential gain of $110.
This is loss aversion overriding expected value calculation.

questions.q: What does the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility state?
options: Total utility decreases as consumption increases.
The price of a good falls as more of it is consumed.
The additional satisfaction from each extra unit of a good decreases as consumption increases.
Consumers always prefer variety over quantity.
questions.explanation: Diminishing marginal utility means the first unit of something is the most valuable, and each additional unit adds less satisfaction than the one before. This is a universal pattern in consumer behavior.

questions.q: What is consumer surplus?
options: The extra goods a consumer buys beyond their needs.
The income left over after all purchases.
The difference between the maximum price a consumer would pay and the actual price they pay.
The utility gained from the last unit purchased.
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus represents value captured by the buyer. If you would pay $80 for something priced at $50, you receive $30 in surplus value. Markets generate consumer surplus, which is part of why free exchange benefits both parties.

questions.q: What is loss aversion in behavioral economics?
options: The tendency to avoid buying insurance.
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains.
The preference for certain outcomes over uncertain ones.
The tendency to avoid all financial risk.
questions.explanation: Loss aversion means the psychological pain of losing $100 is approximately twice as strong as the pleasure of gaining $100. This causes people to make decisions that deviate from rational expected value calculations.

questions.q: What happens to a consumer's budget constraint when their income increases?
options: It rotates around one axis.
It shifts outward, allowing more of all goods to be purchased.
It shifts inward, reducing purchasing ability.
It becomes steeper.
questions.explanation: An increase in income shifts the budget constraint outward in parallel, because the consumer can now afford more of every good at the same prices. The slope (determined by relative prices) does not change.

questions.q: What is the anchoring bias?
options: The tendency to stay committed to a bad decision.
The tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information encountered when making a decision.
The tendency to prefer familiar brands.
The inability to compare prices across stores.
questions.explanation: Anchoring means the first number or piece of information you see disproportionately influences your judgment. Retailers exploit this with original prices crossed out next to sale prices.

lessons.title: Protocol: Utility Maximization
lessons.prompt:
You have $10 to spend. Apples cost $2 each and give 10 units of marginal utility per apple. Bananas cost $1 each and give 4 units of marginal utility per banana.

To maximize utility, should you spend your entire budget on apples, entire budget on bananas, or a mix?

Hint: calculate marginal utility per dollar for each.

Write: apples, bananas, or mix.

validation.message: error: apples give 10 utility per $2 = 5 utility per dollar. bananas give 4 utility per $1 = 4 utility per dollar. apples give more utility per dollar spent, so you should spend everything on apples to maximize utility.

lessons.title: How Firms Produce
lessons.content:
A firm combines inputs to produce outputs. The main categories of inputs are labor (workers), capital (machinery and equipment), land, and raw materials. The production function describes the relationship between inputs and the quantity of output produced.

In the short run, at least one input is fixed and cannot be changed. A factory cannot be built or demolished instantly. The short run is defined not by a specific number of months but by the presence of at least one fixed input.

In the long run, all inputs are variable. Firms can expand their facilities, enter or exit industries, and adjust every factor of production.

The Law of Diminishing Returns applies in the short run. It states that as you add more of a variable input (like labor) to a fixed input (like a factory), the marginal product of that variable input eventually declines.

Marginal product is the additional output generated by one more unit of input. When you hire the first worker in an empty factory, output rises substantially. As you add more workers, they help each other and specialize, so marginal product rises at first. But eventually the factory gets crowded, workers get in each other's way, and each additional worker adds less than the previous one. That is diminishing returns.

This does not mean output falls. It means the rate of increase in output slows.

examples.label: Diminishing Returns to Labor
examples.code:
Workers in a factory with 10 machines:

1 worker: Output = 10 units. Marginal product = 10.
2 workers: Output = 22 units. Marginal product = 12.
3 workers: Output = 36 units. Marginal product = 14.
4 workers: Output = 48 units. Marginal product = 12.
5 workers: Output = 58 units. Marginal product = 10.
6 workers: Output = 64 units. Marginal product = 6.
7 workers: Output = 67 units. Marginal product = 3.

Marginal product peaks at 3 workers, then diminishes.
Total output keeps rising but slower and slower.

lessons.title: Types of Costs
lessons.content:
Understanding costs is central to understanding how firms make decisions. Costs have multiple categories that mean different things in different situations.

Fixed costs (FC) do not change with the level of output. Rent on a factory, loan payments on equipment, salaries of permanent staff. Whether you produce 0 units or 1000 units, fixed costs stay the same in the short run.

Variable costs (VC) change with the level of output. Raw materials, hourly labor, electricity used in production. When output doubles, variable costs roughly double as well.

Total cost (TC) is the sum of fixed and variable costs: TC = FC + VC.

Average fixed cost (AFC) is fixed cost divided by quantity. As output rises, fixed costs are spread over more units, so AFC falls continuously.

Average variable cost (AVC) is variable cost divided by quantity. Due to diminishing returns, AVC eventually rises as output increases.

Average total cost (ATC) is total cost divided by quantity. It has a U-shape because AFC pulls it down at first, but eventually rising AVC dominates.

Marginal cost (MC) is the additional cost of producing one more unit. Due to diminishing returns, MC eventually rises as output increases. The MC curve crosses the ATC curve at the ATC's minimum point. This is always true and is important for understanding firm decisions.

examples.label: Cost Structure Example
examples.code:
A bakery has fixed costs of $1,000/month.

Quantity | VC   | TC    | ATC    | MC
0       | $0   | $1000 |   --   | --
100     | $400 | $1400 | $14.00 | $4
200     | $900 | $1900 | $9.50  | $5
300     | $1500| $2500 | $8.33  | $6
400     | $2400| $3400 | $8.50  | $9
500     | $3800| $4800 | $9.60  | $14

ATC is minimized between 300 and 400 units.
MC is rising and crosses ATC at its minimum.

lessons.title: Economies of Scale
lessons.content:
Economies of scale occur in the long run when increasing the scale of production lowers the average total cost per unit. If a firm doubles all inputs and output more than doubles, it has economies of scale.

Sources of economies of scale include: specialization and division of labor at larger scale, the ability to spread fixed costs of machinery over more units, bulk purchasing discounts on inputs, and more efficient use of capital at large volumes.

Diseconomies of scale occur when increasing scale raises average costs. This happens due to management complexity, coordination problems, and bureaucracy in very large organizations. When every department needs a committee and every decision requires approval from multiple layers, things slow down and costs rise.

Constant returns to scale occur when doubling inputs doubles output, leaving average costs unchanged.

The minimum efficient scale is the output level at which a firm first reaches the lowest point on its long-run average cost curve. Industries where minimum efficient scale is very large relative to market demand tend toward having few, very large firms. This is a natural explanation for industry concentration that does not require any anti-competitive behavior.

examples.label: Economies vs Diseconomies
examples.code:
A small bakery makes 100 loaves/day. ATC = $3/loaf.
It expands to 1,000 loaves/day. ATC = $1.80/loaf.
Economies of scale: bigger is cheaper per unit.

A massive corporation grows to 100,000 employees.
Coordination costs explode. Every decision takes months.
ATC rises. Diseconomies of scale have set in.

The efficient firm size is where ATC is minimized
before diseconomies outweigh economies.

questions.q: What is the Law of Diminishing Returns?
options: As a firm produces more output, total costs rise.
As more of a variable input is added to a fixed input, the marginal product of the variable input eventually declines.
As a firm grows larger, its average costs always fall.
As price rises, quantity supplied falls.
questions.explanation: Diminishing returns is a short-run phenomenon. With at least one fixed input, adding more of the variable input eventually yields smaller and smaller additions to output. The factory gets crowded. Productivity per worker falls.

questions.q: What happens to average fixed cost as output increases?
options: It rises because fixed costs grow with output.
It stays constant because fixed costs do not change.
It falls because the same fixed costs are spread across more units of output.
It rises then falls in a U-shape.
questions.explanation: Fixed costs do not change with output. So as output increases, the same fixed cost is divided by a larger and larger number. Average fixed cost falls continuously as output rises.

questions.q: At which output level does the marginal cost curve cross the average total cost curve?
options: Where ATC is rising.
Where ATC is at its minimum.
Where MC equals zero.
Where ATC equals AFC.
questions.explanation: When MC is below ATC, it pulls the average down. When MC is above ATC, it pulls the average up. Therefore MC must equal ATC at the exact minimum of the ATC curve. This is a mathematical necessity.

questions.q: What is the difference between the short run and long run in production theory?
options: Short run is less than one year; long run is more than one year.
In the short run at least one input is fixed; in the long run all inputs can be varied.
Short run refers to small firms; long run refers to large firms.
The short run involves lower costs than the long run.
questions.explanation: Short and long run are defined by input flexibility, not clock time. In the short run, some inputs (typically capital) are fixed. In the long run, firms can adjust all inputs, including their scale of operations.

questions.q: A firm experiences economies of scale. This means that as it grows:
options: Average costs rise.
Fixed costs increase.
Average costs fall as output increases.
Marginal costs become constant.
questions.explanation: Economies of scale mean that expanding production lowers the average cost per unit. Larger scale allows specialization, bulk purchasing, and more efficient use of capital, all of which reduce per-unit costs.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify the Cost Type
lessons.prompt:
A restaurant has the following monthly expenses:
- Building lease: $3,000
- Chef salary (fixed): $4,000
- Food ingredients: $8,000 (varies with meals served)
- Electricity for cooking: $1,200 (varies with meals served)

What are the total fixed costs per month? Write only the dollar amount.

validation.message: error: fixed costs are the building lease ($3,000) and the chef salary ($4,000). ingredients and electricity vary with output, so they are variable costs. total fixed costs = $7,000.

lessons.title: Perfect Competition
lessons.content:
Perfect competition is a market structure characterized by many buyers and sellers, identical (homogeneous) products, perfect information, free entry and exit from the market, and no single buyer or seller large enough to influence the price.

In perfect competition, individual firms are price takers. They cannot charge above the market price because consumers can instantly switch to identical products from other sellers. They have no incentive to charge below market price because they can sell everything they produce at the market price.

A perfectly competitive firm maximizes profit by producing the quantity where price equals marginal cost (P = MC). Since price equals marginal revenue in perfect competition, this is the same as the profit-maximizing rule of MR = MC.

In the short run, a firm may make economic profit, break even, or make a loss. In the long run, profits attract entry by new firms, which increases supply and pushes price down until economic profit reaches zero. Losses cause firms to exit, reducing supply and pushing price back up to break even.

The long-run equilibrium in perfect competition is where P = MC = minimum ATC. This is the most efficient market structure: price equals marginal cost (allocative efficiency) and firms operate at minimum average cost (productive efficiency).

Examples of near-perfect competition include agricultural commodity markets such as wheat or corn, where the product is essentially identical across producers.

examples.label: Long-Run Adjustment in Perfect Competition
examples.code:
Initially: Price = $10, ATC = $7. Firms earn profit of $3/unit.
New firms enter the industry, attracted by profit.
Supply increases. Market price falls.
Process continues until: Price = ATC = $7.
Economic profit = $0. No further incentive to enter.

If losses occur: Price = $5, ATC = $7. Loss = -$2/unit.
Firms exit the industry. Supply decreases.
Market price rises.
Process continues until: Price = ATC = $7.
Losses eliminated. No further incentive to exit.

lessons.title: Monopoly
lessons.content:
A monopoly exists when one firm is the sole seller of a product with no close substitutes. The monopolist is not a price taker. It is a price maker. It faces the entire market demand curve and can set the price, but it must accept the lower quantity demanded that comes with a higher price.

Sources of monopoly power include: exclusive ownership of a key resource, patents and copyrights, government licensing, and natural monopoly where one firm can supply the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms could.

A natural monopoly arises when a single firm has such large economies of scale that its average costs keep falling over the entire range of market demand. It makes economic sense to have one firm rather than many. Utilities like water and electricity are classic examples.

The monopolist maximizes profit by producing where MR = MC, then charging the highest price consumers will pay for that quantity, found by looking at the demand curve.

Monopoly produces less output and charges a higher price than perfect competition. This creates deadweight loss: transactions that would have occurred in a competitive market do not occur under monopoly because the monopolist restricts output. Deadweight loss represents a net loss to society.

Price discrimination occurs when a monopolist charges different prices to different consumers based on their willingness to pay. A perfect price discriminating monopolist captures all consumer surplus, eliminates deadweight loss, and maximizes its own profit.

examples.label: Monopoly vs Competition
examples.code:
Perfectly competitive outcome:
Price = $10, Quantity = 1,000 units
Price equals marginal cost. No deadweight loss.

Monopoly outcome (same cost structure):
Price = $16, Quantity = 600 units
Monopolist restricts output to raise price.

Losers: consumers who pay more and those
who are priced out of the market entirely.
Deadweight loss: 400 units of beneficial
transactions that no longer occur.

lessons.title: Oligopoly and Monopolistic Competition
lessons.content:
Most real markets fall between the extremes of perfect competition and monopoly.

An oligopoly is a market with a small number of large firms whose decisions are interdependent. Each firm must consider how rivals will respond to its pricing and output decisions. This strategic interdependence is what makes oligopoly analysis require game theory.

The prisoner's dilemma is a classic game theory model that explains why oligopolists often fail to cooperate even when cooperation would benefit all of them. If two firms could collude to both charge high prices, they would both earn high profits. But each firm has an individual incentive to undercut the other and capture more market share. If both follow that incentive, both end up charging lower prices and earning lower profits than if they had cooperated. This is why explicit price-fixing agreements (cartels) form, and why they are illegal.

An oligopoly market can be highly concentrated (three or four dominant firms) or moderately concentrated. Examples include commercial aviation, smartphones, and large supermarket chains.

Monopolistic competition involves many firms selling differentiated products. Each firm has some price-setting power because its product is slightly different from rivals, but the market is competitive enough that economic profits are competed away in the long run. Restaurants, clothing brands, and hair salons operate in monopolistically competitive markets.

In monopolistic competition, firms engage in non-price competition: advertising, branding, product design, and service quality to differentiate themselves and maintain some pricing power.

examples.label: Prisoner's Dilemma in Oligopoly
examples.code:
Two airlines: Fly-Co and Air-X. Both can charge $400 or $250.

If both charge $400: Each earns $1,000,000 profit.
If both charge $250: Each earns $400,000 profit.
If Fly-Co charges $250 while Air-X charges $400:
  Fly-Co captures more passengers: earns $1,200,000.
  Air-X loses passengers: earns $200,000.

Each airline has an incentive to undercut the other.
Result: both end up at $250, earning less than if
they had cooperated at $400.
This is the prisoner's dilemma logic.

questions.q: In perfect competition, why do firms earn zero economic profit in the long run?
options: The government taxes away profits.
Positive economic profits attract new entrants, increasing supply and pushing price down until profits are eliminated.
Firms in perfect competition cannot set their own prices.
Firms voluntarily reduce prices to help consumers.
questions.explanation: Economic profit signals opportunity. In a competitive market with free entry, those profits attract new firms. Increased supply drives down price until the profit opportunity is fully competed away. Long-run equilibrium is zero economic profit.

questions.q: What is a natural monopoly?
options: A monopoly that formed without government help.
A market where one firm can supply the entire demand at lower cost than multiple competing firms due to large economies of scale.
A market dominated by one very talented entrepreneur.
A firm that owns a natural resource.
questions.explanation: Natural monopolies arise when economies of scale are so large that a single firm always has lower average costs than any combination of smaller firms. Forcing competition into such a market raises average costs and is economically inefficient.

questions.q: What is deadweight loss in a monopoly?
options: The profit the monopolist earns at the expense of consumers.
The net loss to society from mutually beneficial transactions that do not occur because the monopolist restricts output.
The cost of government regulation of monopolies.
The reduction in quality that occurs when competition is eliminated.
questions.explanation: Deadweight loss represents destroyed value. Under monopoly, output is restricted below the competitive level. Buyers who would have been willing to pay more than the cost of production are priced out. Those lost transactions represent a net welfare loss to society.

questions.q: What is the key feature that distinguishes oligopoly from other market structures?
options: Firms sell differentiated products.
There is only one seller.
Firms are strategically interdependent: each must consider how rivals will respond to its decisions.
Entry is completely free.
questions.explanation: Oligopoly firms are large enough that each one's decisions directly affect the others. This interdependence means oligopoly analysis requires game theory and models of strategic behavior, unlike competitive markets where firms ignore rivals.

questions.q: How does monopolistic competition differ from perfect competition?
options: Monopolistic competition has fewer buyers.
In monopolistic competition, firms sell differentiated products and have some price-setting power, rather than selling identical products at market price.
Perfect competition involves differentiated products.
Monopolistic competition firms exit when they earn losses.
questions.explanation: Product differentiation is the key difference. In monopolistic competition, each firm's product is slightly different, giving it a small amount of pricing power. In perfect competition, products are identical and firms are pure price takers.

questions.q: Why does collusion between oligopolists tend to break down?
options: Governments always prevent oligopolists from meeting.
Each firm has an individual incentive to undercut the agreed price and capture more market share, even though this makes everyone worse off.
Oligopolists always prefer lower prices.
Collusion is always detected immediately.
questions.explanation: The prisoner's dilemma logic applies. Even when firms agree to charge high prices collectively, each has a private incentive to defect and undercut rivals. When all do this, the cartel collapses and everyone ends up with lower profits than cooperation would have produced.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify Market Structure
lessons.prompt:
A market has the following characteristics:
- 4 large firms control 90% of all sales
- Each firm watches competitor pricing closely before setting its own
- Products are similar but not identical
- Barriers to entry are high

Write the market structure this describes: perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, or monopolistic competition.

validation.message: error: a small number of large firms with strategic interdependence, high barriers to entry, and significant market concentration is the definition of oligopoly.

lessons.title: Supply and Demand in Labor Markets
lessons.content:
Labor is a factor of production bought and sold in markets. Like goods markets, labor markets are governed by supply and demand, but with some important differences.

The demand for labor is derived demand: it comes from the demand for the goods and services that labor produces. Firms hire workers not because they want workers but because they need workers to produce things buyers want. When demand for a product rises, demand for the labor that makes it also rises.

A firm's demand for labor depends on the marginal revenue product of labor (MRP), which is the additional revenue generated by hiring one more worker. MRP equals the marginal product of labor times the price of the output. Firms hire workers as long as the MRP of each worker exceeds the wage they must pay.

The supply of labor in any particular market comes from workers who choose to offer their services at various wage levels. Higher wages attract more workers into a market. Workers face a tradeoff between work (income) and leisure.

The equilibrium wage is where quantity of labor demanded equals quantity of labor supplied. Factors that shift labor demand include changes in product demand, productivity, and the prices of other inputs. Factors that shift labor supply include changes in population, immigration, education levels, and wages in alternative occupations.

Wage differentials across occupations reflect differences in skill requirements, working conditions, education and training costs, geographic location, and the compensating differentials workers require for unpleasant or dangerous jobs.

examples.label: Marginal Revenue Product
examples.code:
A factory sells chairs for $50 each.

Worker 1 produces 10 chairs/day.
MRP = 10 x $50 = $500/day.
If wage is $300/day, hiring is profitable.

Worker 5 produces 4 chairs/day (diminishing returns).
MRP = 4 x $50 = $200/day.
If wage is $300/day, not profitable to hire worker 5.

The firm hires up to the worker whose MRP = wage.

lessons.title: Human Capital and Wage Inequality
lessons.content:
Human capital is the stock of knowledge, skills, and education that workers accumulate. Just as physical capital (machines, factories) makes workers more productive, human capital increases a worker's productivity and earning potential.

Investment in human capital includes formal education, vocational training, on-the-job experience, and health maintenance. These investments have costs (tuition, time) and returns (higher lifetime earnings). Human capital theory predicts that workers who invest more in education should earn higher wages because they are more productive.

The college wage premium is the difference in earnings between college graduates and high school graduates. This premium has grown substantially over recent decades, reflecting increased demand for skilled workers relative to unskilled workers.

Wage inequality is driven by multiple factors: technological change that complements high-skill workers (computers benefit analysts more than assembly line workers), globalization (which puts domestic low-skill workers in competition with lower-wage foreign workers), declining union membership, and differences in human capital accumulation.

Discrimination is another source of wage inequality. When workers of equal productivity are paid differently based on race, gender, or other characteristics unrelated to productivity, this is labor market discrimination. It both reduces individual welfare and creates economic inefficiency by misallocating labor.

examples.label: Return on Education Investment
examples.code:
4-year college degree:
Cost: $120,000 tuition + $140,000 opportunity cost = $260,000

Average wage premium over career: $25,000/year more
than high school graduate.

Over 30 years: $750,000 extra earnings.

Return on investment: ($750,000 - $260,000) / $260,000
= approximately 188% return over a career.

This is why economists treat education as an investment,
not just consumption.

lessons.title: Unions and the Minimum Wage
lessons.content:
Labor unions are organizations of workers that collectively bargain with employers for wages, benefits, and working conditions. By bargaining collectively, workers have more leverage than they would as individuals.

Unions can raise wages for their members above the market equilibrium. This benefits union members but can reduce overall employment in the unionized sector if wages rise above the market-clearing level. The wage differential between union and non-union workers is a subject of substantial economic research.

From the employer's perspective, unions raise labor costs and reduce flexibility. From the worker's perspective, they provide wage stability and protection against arbitrary dismissal. The economic effect depends on market structure: in a competitive labor market, union wage floors cause unemployment; in a monopsony (where one employer dominates), unions may actually increase both wages and employment.

The minimum wage is a government-set price floor in the labor market. Standard supply and demand analysis predicts that a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage creates unemployment: employers hire fewer workers at the higher mandated wage. However, empirical research on the minimum wage shows more complex results, particularly in labor markets where employers have some market power. The employment effects depend on how far above the market wage the minimum is set, the elasticity of labor demand, and local market conditions.

examples.label: Minimum Wage Analysis
examples.code:
Labor market for restaurant workers:
Equilibrium wage: $12/hour
Government minimum wage: $15/hour

At $15/hour:
Quantity of labor demanded: 800,000 workers
Quantity of labor supplied: 1,100,000 workers

Surplus of labor (unemployment): 300,000 workers
want jobs at $15/hour but cannot find them.

Counter-argument: If employers had monopsony power,
they were paying below competitive wages anyway.
A minimum wage could move them closer to
the efficient outcome, not away from it.

questions.q: What is derived demand in the context of labor markets?
options: Demand for labor that comes from government programs.
Demand for labor that is derived from consumer demand for the goods and services that labor produces.
Demand for labor that increases as wages fall.
Demand for skilled labor that is higher than demand for unskilled labor.
questions.explanation: Labor demand is not direct. Firms hire workers to produce goods that consumers want. When demand for those goods rises, demand for the workers who make them also rises. The demand is derived from the product market.

questions.q: What is the marginal revenue product of labor?
options: The average revenue per worker.
The total revenue generated by all workers.
The additional revenue a firm earns by hiring one more worker.
The wage a firm must pay to attract one more worker.
questions.explanation: MRP is the revenue contribution of the marginal worker. Firms hire workers as long as MRP exceeds the wage. When MRP equals the wage, the firm has hired the profit-maximizing number of workers.

questions.q: What is human capital?
options: The financial investments workers make in the stock market.
The physical strength and health of a workforce.
The knowledge, skills, and education workers accumulate that increase their productivity.
The number of workers available in an economy.
questions.explanation: Human capital is the productivity-enhancing investment embodied in a worker. Like physical capital, it is accumulated through investment (education, training, experience) and generates returns over time in the form of higher wages.

questions.q: A minimum wage is set below the market equilibrium wage. What is the effect?
options: It raises wages for all workers.
It creates unemployment because employers hire fewer workers.
It has no effect because it is non-binding.
It creates a shortage of workers.
questions.explanation: A minimum wage below the market equilibrium wage is non-binding. The market wage is already above the legal minimum, so the law changes nothing. Only a minimum wage set above the equilibrium wage has any effect.

lessons.title: Protocol: MRP Hiring Decision
lessons.prompt:
A firm sells output at $20 per unit. Workers earn $100 per day.

Worker 3 produces 6 units per day.
Worker 4 produces 4 units per day.
Worker 5 produces 3 units per day.

How many workers should the firm hire to maximize profit? Write only the number.

validation.message: error: worker 3 MRP = 6 x $20 = $120 > $100 wage. hire. worker 4 MRP = 4 x $20 = $80 < $100 wage. do not hire. the firm should hire exactly 3 workers. wait, recalculate: worker 3 MRP = $120 > $100, hire. worker 4 MRP = 4 x $20 = $80 < $100, do not hire. correct answer is 3.

lessons.title: Externalities
lessons.content:
An externality occurs when a transaction between a buyer and seller affects a third party who is not part of the transaction. The affected third party did not agree to the cost or benefit and receives no compensation or payment for it.

A negative externality imposes a cost on third parties. A factory that pollutes a river imposes costs on everyone downstream who uses that water. These costs are external to the firm's private cost calculation, so the firm produces more than is socially optimal.

A positive externality confers a benefit on third parties. When you get vaccinated, you reduce the risk of infecting others, creating a benefit they did not pay for. This external benefit means the private demand for vaccines is lower than the socially optimal level, leading to underproduction and underconsumption.

The market fails to achieve efficiency in the presence of externalities because private costs and benefits diverge from social costs and benefits. The social optimum requires that all costs and benefits, including external ones, are accounted for.

Government responses to externalities include: Pigouvian taxes (taxing negative externalities to raise private costs to equal social costs), subsidies (for positive externalities to increase quantity toward the social optimum), regulations that directly limit harmful activities, and the creation of tradable permits (cap and trade systems).

The Coase Theorem states that if property rights are well-defined and bargaining costs are low, private parties can negotiate their way to the socially efficient outcome without government intervention. This only works when the affected parties are few and identifiable.

examples.label: Negative Externality: Carbon Emissions
examples.code:
A power plant produces electricity.
Private cost: fuel, labor, equipment = $0.06/kWh
External cost: climate damage from emissions = $0.04/kWh
Social cost = $0.10/kWh

Without intervention:
The plant prices at $0.06 and produces too much.
The external cost is borne by society, not the plant.

Pigouvian tax of $0.04/kWh:
Raises the plant's private cost to $0.10/kWh.
Reduces output to the socially optimal level.
Internalizes the externality.

lessons.title: Public Goods and Common Resources
lessons.content:
Goods can be classified along two dimensions: excludability (can people who do not pay be prevented from using it?) and rivalry (does one person's use reduce availability for others?).

A private good is both excludable and rival. A sandwich: if you eat it, I cannot, and I can be excluded from it if I do not pay.

A public good is neither excludable nor rival. National defense protects everyone within a country simultaneously, and one person's protection does not reduce protection for others. Because public goods are non-excludable, the free rider problem arises: rational individuals have no incentive to pay for them voluntarily, since they cannot be excluded from enjoying them regardless.

The free rider problem means public goods tend to be underprovided by private markets. This provides a rationale for government provision financed by taxes. Everyone is forced to contribute through taxation, solving the free rider problem.

A common resource is rival but non-excludable. A fishery in international waters: your fishing reduces fish available to me, but neither of us can be excluded from fishing. Without coordination, common resources tend to be overexploited. This is the Tragedy of the Commons: individually rational behavior leads to collectively irrational outcomes.

Solutions to the Tragedy of the Commons include government regulation of extraction, privatization (assigning property rights so the owner has incentive to manage sustainably), and community-based governance arrangements.

examples.label: Good Classification
examples.code:
National defense: Non-excludable, non-rival = Public good
(free rider problem, government provides)

A sandwich: Excludable, rival = Private good
(markets work well)

A crowded highway: Non-excludable, rival = Common resource
(congestion = tragedy of commons)

Cable TV (with subscription): Excludable, non-rival = Club good
(natural monopoly tendency, often private)

lessons.title: Information Problems
lessons.content:
Markets work best when both buyers and sellers have full information. When one side of a transaction has significantly more information than the other, market failure can result.

Adverse selection occurs before a transaction, when hidden information causes markets to function poorly. In health insurance, people with health problems are more likely to buy insurance than healthy people. Insurers cannot perfectly observe each customer's health status. The mix of customers is therefore worse than the overall population. This forces insurers to raise premiums, which drives out healthier people, worsening the pool further. In extreme cases, markets can unravel entirely.

George Akerlof's market for lemons shows this with used cars. Sellers know the true quality of their car; buyers do not. Buyers therefore offer only average quality prices. Sellers of high-quality cars refuse to sell at average prices. Only below-average quality cars remain for sale. Buyers anticipate this and lower their offer further. The market fails to sell high-quality used cars.

Moral hazard occurs after a transaction, when having insurance or a guarantee changes behavior. People drive more recklessly when they have comprehensive car insurance. Banks take more risks when they believe the government will bail them out. The insurance removes the full consequences of risky behavior.

Solutions to information problems include signaling (actions taken by the informed party to credibly communicate their type), screening (devices used by the uninformed party to sort among types), and mandatory disclosure requirements.

examples.label: Signaling in Labor Markets
examples.code:
Employers cannot directly observe a job applicant's productivity.
This is an information asymmetry problem.

A college degree serves as a signal.
Not because education always directly increases productivity,
but because completing college is harder for lower-ability
people than higher-ability people.

The signal separates types: employers use degree
as a screening device. High-ability workers invest
in the signal to distinguish themselves from low-ability workers.

This is signaling, not necessarily human capital formation.

questions.q: What is a negative externality?
options: A situation where markets produce too little of a good.
A cost imposed on third parties not involved in a transaction, which the market does not account for.
A government tax that reduces market output.
A situation where price is below equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Negative externalities mean social costs exceed private costs. Producers do not account for the harm they impose on others, so they produce more than is socially optimal. Pollution is the classic example.

questions.q: Why are public goods underprovided by private markets?
options: They are too expensive to produce.
They have no value to consumers.
Because they are non-excludable, individuals can free ride and enjoy the good without paying, so the private market cannot profitably provide them.
Because the government always provides them.
questions.explanation: The free rider problem means rational individuals will not voluntarily pay for public goods they can enjoy regardless. Without payment, private firms cannot profit. Markets underprovide public goods, which is why governments typically provide them through tax-funded mechanisms.

questions.q: What is the Tragedy of the Commons?
options: When governments fail to manage public parks properly.
When individually rational behavior in a common resource leads to collective overexploitation and depletion.
When monopolies control common goods.
When public goods are overproduced.
questions.explanation: A common resource is rival but non-excludable. Each individual has incentive to use as much as possible before others do, because restraint by one person simply allows others to use more. Rational individual behavior leads to collective ruin.

questions.q: What is adverse selection?
options: When insurers select the healthiest customers.
When bad decisions are made by inexperienced managers.
When hidden information before a transaction causes markets to attract disproportionately the worst risks.
When monopolists charge different prices to different customers.
questions.explanation: Adverse selection is a pre-transaction information problem. In insurance, those most likely to need coverage are most likely to buy it, while healthy people opt out. The insured pool is worse than the general population, leading to high premiums and potential market unraveling.

questions.q: A government imposes a tax equal to the external cost per unit of pollution on a polluting factory. What is this policy called?
options: A minimum wage
A price ceiling
A Pigouvian tax
A trade tariff
questions.explanation: A Pigouvian tax is designed to internalize a negative externality. By raising the firm's private cost to equal the social cost, the tax induces the firm to produce the socially optimal quantity without requiring direct regulation of output.

lessons.title: Protocol: Externality Direction
lessons.prompt:
A beekeeper sets up hives next to an apple orchard. The bees pollinate the apple trees, increasing the orchard's apple yield at no cost to the orchard owner.

Is this a positive or negative externality? Who receives it?

Write: positive or negative.

validation.message: error: the orchard receives an uncompensated benefit from the beekeeper's bees. this is a positive externality. the beekeeper and their customers are the transacting parties; the orchard owner is the third party receiving a benefit they did not pay for.

lessons.title: Gross Domestic Product
lessons.content:
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period of time, usually a year or quarter.

The word 'final' matters. GDP counts the sale of a loaf of bread, not the sale of the flour used to make it. Counting the flour would be double-counting: the flour's value is already embedded in the bread's price.

GDP can be measured three ways that produce the same result. The expenditure approach adds up all spending on final goods: consumption (C), investment (I), government spending (G), and net exports (X - M). So GDP = C + I + G + (X - M).

Consumption is household spending on goods and services. Investment is spending by firms on capital goods and inventories (not financial investment). Government spending includes federal, state, and local government purchases but not transfer payments like Social Security. Net exports is exports minus imports.

Nominal GDP is measured in current prices. Real GDP adjusts for inflation to allow comparisons across time. If nominal GDP rose 5% but prices rose 5%, real GDP did not grow. Real GDP growth measures actual increases in output.

GDP per capita is GDP divided by population. It is a rough measure of average living standards. It does not measure distribution of income, environmental quality, leisure, or overall human wellbeing, which is why economists increasingly supplement it with other measures.

examples.label: GDP Expenditure Components
examples.code:
Hypothetical economy in one year:

Consumption (C): $14 trillion
(households buying food, cars, services)

Investment (I): $3.5 trillion
(firms buying equipment, building factories, inventory)

Government (G): $3.5 trillion
(government buying military equipment, road construction)

Net Exports (X-M): -$0.5 trillion
(imports exceed exports by $0.5 trillion)

GDP = 14 + 3.5 + 3.5 + (-0.5) = $20.5 trillion

lessons.title: Unemployment
lessons.content:
The unemployment rate is the percentage of the labor force that is not employed but is actively seeking work. To be counted as unemployed, a person must be without a job and actively looking for one. People who have given up looking are not counted in the labor force and therefore do not appear in the unemployment rate.

The labor force participation rate measures the fraction of the adult population that is either employed or actively seeking employment. It captures how engaged the working-age population is with the labor market.

There are three main types of unemployment. Frictional unemployment is the natural result of people moving between jobs. Workers who quit to find a better job, or new graduates searching for their first position, are frictionally unemployed. Some frictional unemployment is healthy because it reflects a dynamic economy.

Structural unemployment occurs when a mismatch develops between worker skills and the skills employers need. Technological change can make entire job categories obsolete, leaving workers with outdated skills unemployed even when jobs exist elsewhere. Structural unemployment requires retraining and adjustment over time.

Cyclical unemployment occurs due to downturns in the overall economy. When GDP contracts, firms cut production and lay off workers. This unemployment is temporary in theory, declining as the economy recovers.

The natural rate of unemployment is the unemployment rate that prevails when the economy is at full employment: it includes frictional and structural unemployment but not cyclical. It is not zero. Most economists estimate the natural rate is around 4-5% for the United States.

examples.label: Types of Unemployment Examples
examples.code:
Frictional:
A nurse quits her hospital job to find one closer to home.
She is job searching for 3 weeks.
This is normal labor market turnover.

Structural:
A coal miner loses his job as the region switches
to renewable energy. His skills do not transfer easily.
He needs retraining for a different sector.

Cyclical:
An autoworker is laid off during a recession
because car sales plummeted.
He would be rehired if the economy recovered.

lessons.title: Inflation and Price Levels
lessons.content:
Inflation is a sustained rise in the overall price level in an economy. When the general price level rises, each dollar buys fewer goods and services. The purchasing power of money falls.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is the most commonly used measure of inflation. It tracks the price of a representative basket of goods and services purchased by a typical household over time.

The inflation rate is the percentage change in the CPI over a period. If the CPI was 260 last year and is 267 this year, the inflation rate is (267-260)/260 = 2.7%.

Moderate inflation (around 2%) is generally considered healthy. It gives central banks room to cut interest rates during recessions, and mild inflation encourages spending over holding cash.

Deflation (falling prices) sounds good but is often damaging. When prices fall, consumers delay purchases expecting further price drops. This reduces spending, which reduces production, which lowers prices further. This deflationary spiral can be very difficult to escape, as Japan experienced in the 1990s.

Hyperinflation is extremely rapid inflation that destroys the value of money and can collapse an economy. Germany in the 1920s and Zimbabwe in the 2000s are historical examples.

Inflation hurts those on fixed incomes and creditors (who are repaid in dollars worth less than when they lent). It benefits debtors (who repay in cheaper dollars) and owners of real assets.

examples.label: Who Wins and Loses from Inflation
examples.code:
You lend $10,000 at 3% interest for 10 years.
Inflation averages 5% per year.

Real interest rate = nominal rate - inflation rate
= 3% - 5% = -2%

In real terms, the money you get back is worth less
than what you lent. The borrower wins.
The lender loses.

Unexpected inflation redistributes wealth from creditors
to debtors and from those on fixed incomes
to those with assets whose value rises with prices.

lessons.title: The Business Cycle
lessons.content:
Economies do not grow at a steady pace. They fluctuate between periods of expansion and contraction. These fluctuations are called the business cycle.

A recession is a period of significant decline in economic activity across the economy, lasting more than a few months. Technically, it is often defined as two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth. During recessions, GDP falls, unemployment rises, and business investment contracts.

A depression is a severe, prolonged recession. The Great Depression of the 1930s saw US GDP fall by about 30% and unemployment reach 25%.

An expansion or recovery is a period of rising economic activity. GDP grows, unemployment falls, and consumer and business confidence improve.

A peak is the top of the cycle just before a downturn begins. A trough is the bottom of the cycle just before recovery begins.

The causes of business cycles are debated. They can be triggered by demand shocks (sudden changes in spending), supply shocks (sudden changes in production costs, like an oil price spike), financial crises, or changes in consumer and business confidence.

Automatic stabilizers are features of the tax and spending system that automatically reduce the severity of recessions without requiring deliberate government action.
