Unemployment insurance automatically pays out more during recessions, supporting consumer spending. Taxes automatically fall as incomes fall, leaving more money in private hands.

examples.label: Business Cycle Phases
examples.code:
Expansion:
GDP growing, unemployment falling,
business investment rising, consumer confidence high.

Peak:
Economy at its strongest. GDP growth may slow.
Inflation pressure may build as capacity fills.

Recession:
GDP declining, unemployment rising,
investment falling, confidence falling.

Trough:
Economy at its weakest.
Conditions for recovery begin to develop.

Recovery:
GDP begins rising again from the trough.
Slow at first, then accelerating.

questions.q: What does GDP measure?
options: The total income of all citizens of a country regardless of where they work.
The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given time period.
The total value of all goods produced, including intermediate goods.
The total wealth of a country's population.
questions.explanation: GDP counts final goods and services produced within borders during the period. It excludes intermediate goods to avoid double-counting, and counts production within borders (not by citizens abroad).

questions.q: Why is real GDP a better measure of economic growth than nominal GDP?
options: Real GDP grows faster and shows more progress.
Real GDP adjusts for price changes, so it measures actual increases in the quantity of output rather than just increases in prices.
Nominal GDP only counts goods, not services.
Real GDP includes government spending; nominal does not.
questions.explanation: If prices rise but actual production does not, nominal GDP rises without any real improvement. Real GDP strips out the price effect, measuring only genuine changes in the quantity of goods and services produced.

questions.q: A worker has been unemployed for 14 months and has stopped actively searching for work. How is this worker classified?
options: Unemployed, counted in the unemployment rate.
Structurally unemployed.
Not in the labor force, not counted in the unemployment rate.
Frictionally unemployed.
questions.explanation: To be counted as unemployed, a worker must be actively seeking work. Someone who has stopped searching is classified as out of the labor force. This means the official unemployment rate can understate true labor market weakness.

questions.q: What type of unemployment would a travel agent experience after online booking platforms make their job largely obsolete?
options: Frictional unemployment
Cyclical unemployment
Seasonal unemployment
Structural unemployment
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment results from a mismatch between worker skills and employer needs, often caused by technological change. A travel agent whose skills are obsoleted by technology faces structural unemployment that cannot be resolved just by the economy recovering.

questions.q: Who is hurt most by unexpected inflation?
options: Borrowers who repay loans in cheaper dollars.
People who own real estate and other physical assets.
Creditors and people on fixed incomes who receive payments in dollars worth less than expected.
Exporters who sell goods abroad.
questions.explanation: Inflation erodes the real value of fixed nominal payments. Creditors who lend at a fixed rate receive repayment in dollars worth less than when they lent. People on fixed wages or pensions see their purchasing power fall.

lessons.title: Protocol: Calculate GDP
lessons.prompt:
An economy has the following data for one year:

Consumer spending: $800 billion
Business investment: $150 billion
Government purchases: $200 billion
Exports: $100 billion
Imports: $120 billion

Using the expenditure approach, calculate GDP.

Write only the number in billions (example format: $1,000 billion).

lessons.title: Government Spending and Taxation
lessons.content:
Fiscal policy refers to the use of government spending and taxation to influence the overall economy. It is the primary macroeconomic tool available to elected governments.

Expansionary fiscal policy is used during recessions to stimulate economic activity. It involves increasing government spending, decreasing taxes, or both. More government spending directly adds to aggregate demand. Lower taxes leave more money in household and business hands, which tends to increase consumption and investment.

Contractionary fiscal policy is used when the economy is overheating, producing high inflation. It involves decreasing government spending, increasing taxes, or both. This removes money from the economy, reducing spending and slowing the pace of price increases.

The multiplier effect is the phenomenon where an initial change in spending leads to a larger total change in GDP. If the government spends $100 billion, those who receive that money spend some of it, those who receive that spending spend some of it, and so on. The total increase in GDP exceeds the initial $100 billion. The size of the multiplier depends on the marginal propensity to consume: how much of each additional dollar of income people choose to spend rather than save.

Crowding out occurs when government borrowing to finance spending drives up interest rates, reducing private investment. If the government borrows heavily, it competes with private borrowers for available funds, pushing interest rates up. Higher interest rates make private investment projects less attractive and some get cancelled.

examples.label: The Multiplier Effect
examples.code:
Marginal propensity to consume (MPC) = 0.8
Government increases spending by $100 billion.

Round 1: Government spends $100 billion.
Recipients spend 80% = $80 billion.

Round 2: Those recipients spend 80% = $64 billion.

Round 3: $51.2 billion... and so on.

Multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC) = 1 / (1 - 0.8) = 5

Total increase in GDP = $100 billion x 5 = $500 billion.
Initial $100 billion generates $500 billion in total output.

lessons.title: Budget Deficits and National Debt
lessons.content:
A budget deficit occurs when a government spends more than it collects in tax revenue in a given year. A budget surplus occurs when revenue exceeds spending.

When a government runs a deficit, it must borrow to finance the gap. It does this by selling government bonds, which are IOUs promising to repay the borrowed amount plus interest at a future date.

The national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus all past surpluses. It is the total amount the government owes to all its creditors. The United States national debt exceeds $30 trillion.

Whether deficits are harmful depends on context. A small deficit during a recession, when the government is using fiscal stimulus to support a contracting economy, is generally considered acceptable. Persistent large deficits during economic expansions are more concerning because they create long-term debt without the offsetting benefit of fighting a downturn.

Debt sustainability is the key concern. A government can maintain high debt if its economy grows faster than the interest rate it pays on debt. The debt-to-GDP ratio is the standard measure: a country with $30 trillion in debt and $25 trillion in GDP has a debt-to-GDP ratio of 120%.

High debt levels constrain future fiscal policy because a larger share of government revenue must go to interest payments, leaving less for spending or tax cuts when stimulus is needed.

examples.label: Deficit vs Debt
examples.code:
Year 1: Government spends $5 trillion, collects $4 trillion in taxes.
Deficit = $1 trillion. Borrows $1 trillion.
Debt = $1 trillion.

Year 2: Government spends $5.2 trillion, collects $4 trillion.
Deficit = $1.2 trillion. Borrows $1.2 trillion.
Debt = $1 trillion + $1.2 trillion = $2.2 trillion.

Year 3: Surplus of $0.5 trillion (collects $5.7, spends $5.2).
Pays down $0.5 trillion of debt.
Debt = $2.2 trillion - $0.5 trillion = $1.7 trillion.

The deficit is the annual gap. The debt is the running total.

lessons.title: Tax Systems
lessons.content:
Taxes are the primary source of government revenue and a key tool of fiscal policy. How a tax is designed affects both its revenue generation and its economic effects.

A progressive tax takes a higher percentage of income from higher earners. The US federal income tax is progressive: the first bracket of income is taxed at 10%, higher brackets at higher rates. Progressive taxes reduce income inequality.

A regressive tax takes a higher percentage of income from lower earners than from higher earners. Sales taxes are regressive: everyone pays the same percentage on purchases, but since lower-income households spend a larger share of their income, the tax consumes a larger fraction of their income.

A proportional (flat) tax takes the same percentage of income from everyone regardless of income level.

The Laffer Curve describes the relationship between tax rates and tax revenue. At a tax rate of 0%, revenue is zero. At a rate of 100%, revenue is also zero (no one would work if all income were confiscated). In between, there is some rate that maximizes revenue. The debate is about where the current rate is relative to that maximum.

Tax incidence refers to who actually bears the burden of a tax, which may differ from who officially pays it. If a tax is imposed on sellers of a good, sellers may raise prices, shifting some of the burden to buyers. The actual distribution of burden depends on the elasticities of supply and demand.

examples.label: Tax Incidence
examples.code:
Government taxes sellers $2 per gallon of gasoline.
Before tax: price = $3, quantity = 100 gallons.

Sellers pass on $1.50 of the tax by raising price to $4.50.
Sellers absorb $0.50, reducing their net price from $3 to $2.50.

Buyer burden: +$1.50 (price rise)
Seller burden: -$0.50 (lower net price)

The tax burden is split because demand for gas is somewhat
inelastic: buyers cannot easily avoid it.
More inelastic demand = buyers bear more of the tax burden.

questions.q: What is expansionary fiscal policy?
options: Increasing taxes to reduce the deficit.
Increasing government spending or decreasing taxes to stimulate economic activity during a recession.
Raising interest rates to control inflation.
Reducing the money supply to slow economic growth.
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy adds to aggregate demand. By spending more or taxing less, the government puts more money into the economy, supporting output and employment during downturns.

questions.q: What is the multiplier effect?
options: The tendency for taxes to increase faster than government spending.
The phenomenon where an initial change in government spending leads to a larger total change in GDP as money circulates through the economy.
The increase in debt caused by compound interest.
The increase in imports caused by rising incomes.
questions.explanation: Money spent cycles through the economy multiple times. Each recipient spends some fraction, creating income for others, who spend some fraction, and so on. The initial injection generates a multiple of itself in total GDP change.

questions.q: What is the difference between the national debt and the annual budget deficit?
options: They are the same thing measured differently.
The deficit is the annual gap between spending and revenue; the national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus surpluses.
The national debt is paid off each year; the deficit carries forward.
The deficit is owed to foreign governments; the national debt is owed to citizens.
questions.explanation: The deficit is a flow variable: how much more than revenues the government spends in one year. The debt is a stock variable: the total accumulated borrowing over all years, minus any repayment.

questions.q: What does it mean for a tax to be regressive?
options: The tax rate falls as income rises.
Lower-income households pay a higher percentage of their income in tax than higher-income households.
The tax was introduced in a previous era and is now outdated.
The tax is unpopular and politically controversial.
questions.explanation: A regressive tax burden falls more heavily, as a share of income, on lower earners. Even if the rate is the same for everyone, if lower-income people must spend a larger fraction of their income, the tax takes a larger percentage of their income.

questions.q: What is crowding out?
options: When too many firms enter a market and profits fall.
When government borrowing drives up interest rates, reducing private investment.
When inflation reduces the purchasing power of savings.
When imports crowd out domestic production.
questions.explanation: When the government borrows heavily, it competes with private borrowers for available funds. This competition drives up interest rates, which makes private investment more costly and causes some projects to be cancelled.

questions.q: A tax on gasoline is imposed on sellers. Gasoline demand is very inelastic. Who bears most of the tax burden?
options: Sellers, because the tax is officially imposed on them.
Buyers, because inelastic demand means they cannot easily reduce their consumption when prices rise.
The government, because it collects less revenue.
Foreign producers, because domestic producers pass costs abroad.
questions.explanation: Tax incidence depends on elasticity. When demand is inelastic, buyers continue purchasing despite price rises. Sellers can pass most of the tax onto buyers through higher prices. The more inelastic the demand, the more buyers bear the burden.

lessons.title: Protocol: Fiscal Policy Direction
lessons.prompt:
The economy is in a deep recession. GDP has fallen for three consecutive quarters. Unemployment is at 9%.

Should the government use expansionary or contractionary fiscal policy to address this situation?

Write one word: expansionary or contractionary.

validation.message: error: a recession calls for expansionary fiscal policy: increase government spending, cut taxes, or both. this adds to aggregate demand, supporting output and employment. contractionary policy would make the recession worse.

lessons.title: Money and Banking
lessons.content:
Money is anything widely accepted as a medium of exchange. It solves the problem of barter, which requires a double coincidence of wants: both parties must have exactly what the other wants at the same time.

Money performs three functions. It is a medium of exchange (used to buy and sell things), a store of value (can be saved and used later), and a unit of account (a common measure for pricing goods and comparing values).

M1 is the narrowest measure of money supply: physical currency in circulation plus demand deposits (checking accounts). M2 adds savings accounts, money market accounts, and small time deposits.

Commercial banks create money through the money multiplier process. Banks are required to keep a fraction of their deposits as reserves and can lend out the rest. When a bank loans out deposits, those funds are deposited in other banks, which loan out most of that, and so on.

If the reserve requirement is 10%, an initial deposit of $1,000 can support up to $10,000 in total deposits through the banking system. The money multiplier is 1 / reserve requirement.

The Federal Reserve (the Fed) is the central bank of the United States. It regulates the banking system, serves as a lender of last resort to prevent bank failures from cascading, and conducts monetary policy to manage the macroeconomy.

examples.label: Money Multiplier
examples.code:
Reserve requirement = 10% (banks must keep 10% of deposits).
Initial deposit: $1,000.

Bank A keeps $100, lends $900.
Bank B receives $900, keeps $90, lends $810.
Bank C receives $810, keeps $81, lends $729.
...

Total deposits created = $1,000 x (1/0.10) = $10,000.

Money multiplier = 1 / reserve requirement = 10.
A single $1,000 deposit supports $10,000 in total money supply.

lessons.title: How the Fed Controls the Money Supply
lessons.content:
Monetary policy refers to the actions a central bank takes to influence the money supply and interest rates in order to achieve macroeconomic goals like low inflation and full employment.

The Federal Reserve's primary tool is the federal funds rate: the interest rate at which banks lend reserves to each other overnight. By raising or lowering this rate, the Fed influences all other interest rates in the economy.

To lower interest rates (expansionary monetary policy), the Fed buys government bonds from banks in open market operations. This injects reserves into the banking system, increases the money supply, and puts downward pressure on interest rates. Lower rates make borrowing cheaper, encouraging investment and consumption.

To raise interest rates (contractionary monetary policy), the Fed sells government bonds to banks. This removes reserves from the banking system, reduces the money supply, and puts upward pressure on interest rates. Higher rates make borrowing more expensive, slowing spending and reducing inflation pressure.

The discount rate is the interest rate the Fed charges banks for emergency borrowing directly from the Fed. The reserve requirement is the fraction of deposits banks must hold. Both are additional monetary policy tools, though the federal funds rate is the primary lever.

Quantitative easing (QE) is a non-traditional monetary policy tool used when interest rates are already near zero. The Fed purchases large quantities of long-term assets to inject liquidity into the financial system and push down long-term interest rates.

examples.label: Monetary Policy Transmission
examples.code:
Fed lowers federal funds rate from 5% to 3%.

Banks can borrow cheaply from each other.
Banks lower mortgage rates, car loan rates, business loan rates.

Cheaper mortgages: more people buy homes.
More home buying: construction increases, furniture demand rises.

Cheaper business loans: firms invest in new equipment.
More investment: hiring increases, output rises.

Lower interest rates on savings: some people save less, spend more.

Aggregate demand rises. GDP grows. Unemployment falls.

lessons.title: Inflation Targeting and Central Bank Independence
lessons.content:
Most modern central banks operate with a target inflation rate. The Federal Reserve's target is 2% annual inflation. This target provides a clear anchor for expectations: businesses and workers can plan ahead knowing the Fed will act to keep inflation near 2%.

When inflation rises above target, the Fed raises interest rates to cool spending and reduce price pressure. When inflation falls below target or the economy contracts, the Fed lowers interest rates to stimulate activity.

The dual mandate is the Fed's legal obligation to pursue both price stability (low inflation) and maximum employment. These goals sometimes conflict. Fighting inflation by raising rates can increase unemployment. Supporting full employment can risk generating inflation.

Central bank independence means the central bank makes monetary policy decisions without direct control by elected politicians. Independence is considered important because politicians face electoral incentives to stimulate the economy before elections, which can generate inflation. An independent central bank can take unpopular steps like raising rates when necessary without fear of losing an election.

The Phillips Curve describes a short-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment: lower unemployment tends to be associated with higher inflation as a tight labor market pushes up wages and therefore prices. In the long run, many economists believe this tradeoff disappears and unemployment returns to its natural rate regardless of the inflation rate.

examples.label: Fed Response to High Inflation
examples.code:
2022 scenario:
Inflation rises to 8%, well above the 2% target.
Unemployment is at 3.5%, near historic lows.

Fed response: aggressive rate hikes.
Fed funds rate raised from 0.25% to over 5% in 18 months.

Effect:
Borrowing costs rise sharply.
Mortgage rates rise from 3% to over 7%.
Business investment slows.
Consumer spending on credit falls.
Inflation eventually falls toward 3-4%.
Cost: unemployment rises modestly.

questions.q: What are the three functions of money?
options: Store of value, unit of account, medium of exchange.
Asset, liability, equity.
Saving, spending, investment.
Currency, credit, capital.
questions.explanation: Money serves three purposes: it facilitates exchange (medium of exchange), can be stored for future use (store of value), and provides a common measure for prices and comparisons (unit of account). All three are necessary for a monetary economy.

questions.q: When the Federal Reserve buys government bonds in open market operations, what happens to the money supply?
options: The money supply decreases.
The money supply is unchanged.
The money supply increases as reserves are injected into the banking system.
Interest rates rise.
questions.explanation: When the Fed buys bonds, it pays for them by crediting banks' reserve accounts. More reserves support more lending and money creation. The money supply expands, putting downward pressure on interest rates.

questions.q: What is the primary monetary policy tool used by the Federal Reserve?
options: The reserve requirement.
The discount rate.
The federal funds rate, set through open market operations.
The exchange rate.
questions.explanation: The federal funds rate is the Fed's principal lever. By influencing this rate through buying and selling government bonds, the Fed affects all other interest rates in the economy, thereby influencing borrowing, spending, and investment.

questions.q: Why do most economists support central bank independence?
options: Because central banks have better economic forecasts than governments.
Because independent central banks can take necessary but politically unpopular actions like raising rates without electoral consequences.
Because independent central banks have lower operating costs.
Because governments do not understand monetary policy.
questions.explanation: Politicians face incentives to stimulate economies before elections regardless of whether stimulus is appropriate. An independent central bank can raise rates when necessary to fight inflation without worrying about electoral outcomes.

questions.q: What is the short-run tradeoff described by the Phillips Curve?
options: Lower inflation leads to lower interest rates.
Lower unemployment tends to be associated with higher inflation as tight labor markets push up wages.
Higher GDP leads to higher unemployment.
Lower interest rates always reduce inflation.
questions.explanation: The Phillips Curve shows that in the short run, policymakers face a tradeoff: policies that reduce unemployment tend to generate more inflation, and vice versa. The long-run version of the curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment.

questions.q: What is quantitative easing?
options: Printing physical currency to pay government debts.
Reducing the reserve requirement to encourage more lending.
Large-scale central bank purchases of assets to inject liquidity when interest rates are already near zero.
Raising the federal funds rate to reduce inflation.
questions.explanation: Quantitative easing is used when conventional interest rate cuts are exhausted (rates near zero). The central bank buys long-term assets to inject reserves and push down long-term rates, providing stimulus through non-traditional channels.

lessons.title: Protocol: Monetary Policy Direction
lessons.prompt:
Inflation is running at 7%, well above the 2% target. The economy is at full employment.

Should the Federal Reserve use expansionary or contractionary monetary policy?

Write one word: expansionary or contractionary.

validation.message: error: high inflation above target calls for contractionary monetary policy. the Fed raises interest rates to reduce borrowing, slow spending, and bring down price pressure. expansionary policy would add to demand and worsen inflation.

lessons.title: Why Countries Trade
lessons.content:
International trade allows countries to consume beyond what they could produce alone. The theoretical foundation for trade is the principle of comparative advantage.

Absolute advantage means one country can produce a good using fewer resources than another. Comparative advantage means one country can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another. Comparative advantage, not absolute advantage, determines trade patterns.

Even if one country is more efficient at producing everything, both countries can still gain from trade by specializing in what they have a comparative advantage in and trading for the rest. This is because specialization and trade expand the total output available to both countries.

As a simple example: if the US can produce either 10 units of wheat or 5 units of cloth per unit of time, and Mexico can produce either 6 units of wheat or 6 units of cloth, the US has a comparative advantage in wheat (opportunity cost of wheat is 0.5 cloth, versus Mexico's 1 cloth) and Mexico has a comparative advantage in cloth (opportunity cost of cloth is 1 wheat versus US's 2 wheat).

Both countries are better off if the US specializes in wheat, Mexico specializes in cloth, and they trade. Total world output of both goods is higher than if each produced both independently.

The gains from trade are not only theoretical. Empirically, countries that participate more in international trade tend to grow faster, though the distribution of gains is uneven and some groups within countries are harmed by trade even as the country as a whole benefits.

examples.label: Comparative Advantage Example
examples.code:
Country A can produce:
- 100 units of wheat OR 50 units of cloth (1 hour)
Opportunity cost of wheat = 0.5 cloth.
Opportunity cost of cloth = 2 wheat.

Country B can produce:
- 30 units of wheat OR 30 units of cloth (1 hour)
Opportunity cost of wheat = 1 cloth.
Opportunity cost of cloth = 1 wheat.

Country A has comparative advantage in wheat
(lower opportunity cost: 0.5 < 1).

Country B has comparative advantage in cloth
(lower opportunity cost: 1 < 2).

A should specialize in wheat. B should specialize in cloth.
Both trade to get the other good.

lessons.title: Trade Barriers and Protectionism
lessons.content:
Free trade maximizes total output but its benefits are distributed unevenly. This creates political pressure for trade barriers that protect industries and workers facing foreign competition.

A tariff is a tax on imported goods. It raises the price of imports, making domestic goods more competitive. Domestic producers benefit and the government collects revenue. Consumers pay higher prices and total economic welfare generally falls. A deadweight loss is created.

A quota is a limit on the quantity of a good that can be imported. Like a tariff, it raises the domestic price of the good and benefits domestic producers at consumer expense. Unlike a tariff, the government collects no revenue. Instead, whoever holds the import license captures the price difference.

A subsidy to domestic producers helps them compete with imports by lowering their costs. This keeps domestic prices low for consumers but costs the government money.

Arguments for trade barriers include: protecting infant industries that need time to develop before competing globally, protecting strategic industries important for national security, retaliating against trading partners who impose unfair barriers, and preserving jobs in industries facing import competition.

Economists are generally skeptical of protectionist arguments because barriers reduce efficiency, invite retaliation, and protect specific industries at the expense of all consumers who pay higher prices. Trade agreements like the WTO framework seek to reduce barriers through multilateral negotiation.

examples.label: Effects of a Tariff
examples.code:
Before tariff: imported cars sell for $25,000.
Domestic producers sell 100,000 cars at $25,000.

Government imposes $5,000 tariff per imported car.
Imported cars now cost $30,000.

Domestic producers now sell 130,000 cars at $30,000.
(They gain: higher price and more sales.)

Consumers pay $5,000 more per car. They buy fewer cars.

Government collects tariff revenue on remaining imports.

Deadweight loss: transactions that would have happened
without the tariff no longer occur.

lessons.title: Exchange Rates
lessons.content:
An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another. If the exchange rate between dollars and euros is 1.10, one dollar buys 1.10 euros (or equivalently, one euro costs about $0.91).

Exchange rates are determined by supply and demand in foreign exchange markets. The dollar appreciates when demand for dollars rises or supply of dollars falls. It depreciates when demand falls or supply rises.

Factors that affect exchange rates include interest rate differentials between countries, inflation differentials, economic growth rates, political stability, and market expectations.

If US interest rates rise relative to European rates, investors worldwide want to hold more dollar-denominated assets. They demand more dollars, driving up the dollar's value. A stronger dollar makes US exports more expensive for foreigners and makes imports cheaper for Americans. This tends to reduce exports and increase imports.

A weaker dollar makes US exports cheaper for foreign buyers and makes imports more expensive for Americans. This tends to increase exports and decrease imports.

A current account deficit means a country imports more than it exports, spending more abroad than it earns from foreigners. A current account surplus means the opposite. Countries with persistent current account deficits must borrow from abroad or sell assets to finance the gap.

examples.label: Dollar Appreciation Effects
examples.code:
Exchange rate: 1 USD = 100 Japanese yen.
A US car exported to Japan: costs $20,000 = 2,000,000 yen.

Dollar appreciates: 1 USD = 130 Japanese yen.
Same $20,000 car now costs Japanese buyers 2,600,000 yen.
US exports become more expensive. Japan buys fewer US cars.

A Japanese car imported to US: costs 3,000,000 yen.
Before: 3,000,000 / 100 = $30,000
After: 3,000,000 / 130 = $23,077
Imports become cheaper for Americans.

questions.q: What does comparative advantage mean?
options: A country produces more of a good than another.
A country produces a good at lower opportunity cost than another, giving it an advantage in trade.
A country has better technology than another.
A country exports more than it imports.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is about opportunity cost, not absolute efficiency. A country with a lower opportunity cost for producing a good should specialize in it and trade, even if another country can produce it more efficiently in absolute terms.

questions.q: What is the primary economic objection to import tariffs?
options: They create government revenue that could have been avoided.
They help domestic producers at the expense of domestic consumers, reduce efficiency, invite retaliation, and create deadweight loss.
They reduce the domestic price of goods.
They are unfair to foreign governments.
questions.explanation: Tariffs protect domestic producers but do so by raising prices for all domestic consumers. The gains to producers are smaller than the losses to consumers plus deadweight loss. Retaliatory tariffs from trading partners can compound the damage.

questions.q: If the US dollar appreciates relative to foreign currencies, what happens to US exports?
options: US exports become cheaper for foreign buyers and rise.
US exports become more expensive for foreign buyers and tend to fall.
US exports are unaffected because prices are set domestically.
US exports rise because the stronger dollar shows economic strength.
questions.explanation: A stronger dollar means foreigners need more of their currency to buy each dollar. US goods, priced in dollars, become more expensive in foreign currencies. Foreign buyers reduce their purchases of US exports.

questions.q: Why can two countries both gain from trade even if one is absolutely more efficient at producing everything?
options: Because trade is voluntary and both parties agree to it.
Because by specializing in what each country has a comparative advantage in and trading, total world output of all goods increases beyond what self-sufficiency would allow.
Because the less efficient country benefits from learning the other's methods.
Because international law requires gains to be shared equally.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage means specialization increases total output. When each country produces what it can produce at lowest opportunity cost, the combined output exceeds what each would produce alone. Trade allows both to consume more than self-sufficient production would allow.

questions.q: What is a current account deficit?
options: When a government spends more than it collects in taxes.
When a country's imports of goods and services exceed its exports.
When a country has more debt than assets.
When a central bank holds insufficient reserves.
questions.explanation: The current account tracks flows of goods, services, and income between countries. A deficit means the country is buying more from the rest of the world than it is selling to them. This must be financed by borrowing from abroad or selling domestic assets.

lessons.title: Protocol: Comparative Advantage
lessons.prompt:
Country X can produce 80 units of rice or 40 units of steel per day.
Country Y can produce 60 units of rice or 60 units of steel per day.

Which country has a comparative advantage in steel?

Write only the country: X or Y.

validation.message: error: opportunity cost of steel for Country X = 80/40 = 2 units of rice. opportunity cost of steel for Country Y = 60/60 = 1 unit of rice. Country Y gives up fewer units of rice to produce steel. Country Y has the comparative advantage in steel.

lessons.title: Measuring Income Inequality
lessons.content:
Not all households in an economy earn the same income. Understanding the distribution of income requires measures that go beyond average income.

The Lorenz Curve is a graphical representation of income distribution. It plots the cumulative share of income received by the bottom percentage of the population. A perfectly equal distribution would produce a straight 45-degree line: the bottom 20% would have 20% of income, the bottom 40% would have 40%, and so on. Real economies always have a Lorenz Curve that bows below this line.

The Gini coefficient is a number derived from the Lorenz Curve that summarizes inequality in a single figure. It equals the area between the line of equality and the Lorenz Curve divided by the total area below the line of equality. It ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (one person has all income). The US Gini coefficient is around 0.39.

Inequality has increased in most advanced economies over recent decades. The explanations include technological change that raises returns to skilled workers, globalization that competes down wages for unskilled workers, declining union membership, and policies that reduced top marginal tax rates.

Poverty is measured in two ways. Absolute poverty is defined by a fixed income threshold below which a household cannot meet basic needs. The US official poverty line is set at roughly three times a minimum food budget. Relative poverty is defined as falling below some fraction (often 50% or 60%) of the median income. By this measure, poverty is inherently about inequality, not just material deprivation.

examples.label: Gini Coefficient Comparison
examples.code:
Gini coefficients (approximate, recent years):

Norway: 0.26 (relatively equal)
Germany: 0.31
United Kingdom: 0.35
United States: 0.39
China: 0.47
Brazil: 0.52
South Africa: 0.63 (highly unequal)

Higher Gini = more inequality.
Comparisons reveal how much variation exists
in income distribution across countries with
very different policies and histories.

lessons.title: Redistribution and Social Programs
lessons.content:
Governments use taxes and transfers to redistribute income and reduce poverty. The debate over how much redistribution is appropriate involves both empirical questions (what are the economic effects?) and normative questions (what is fair?).

Progressive income taxes take more from high earners, reducing inequality after tax. Transfer payments (Social Security, unemployment insurance, Medicaid, food assistance) raise the income floor for low earners.

The efficiency-equity tradeoff is a central tension in redistribution policy. High taxes on the wealthy may reduce their incentive to work and invest, potentially reducing economic output. Generous transfers may reduce incentives for low-income workers to seek employment. However, empirical evidence on these disincentive effects is mixed and context-dependent. Small transfers to the very poor often have minimal work disincentive effects because recipients still prefer working.

Social insurance programs protect against specific risks. Social Security is insurance against the risk of outliving one's savings. Unemployment insurance protects against the risk of job loss. Health insurance protects against catastrophic medical costs. These programs address market failures in insurance markets where adverse selection would undermine private provision.

The earned income tax credit (EITC) is a refundable tax credit for low-income working households. It supplements wages and creates an incentive to work because the credit increases with earnings up to a threshold. It is broadly supported by economists as an efficient way to redistribute income.

examples.label: Efficiency-Equity Tradeoff
examples.code:
Scenario: A 90% top marginal tax rate.

Equity argument: Very high earners pay most of the tax.
Reversal of a trend toward extreme concentration of income.

Efficiency argument: A person who earns $1,000,000
keeps only $100,000 after tax.
Marginal incentive to earn is greatly reduced.
Some high earners may work less, invest less,
or move to lower-tax countries.
Tax revenue may actually fall (Laffer Curve).

The debate is empirical: how large are these effects?
Evidence suggests moderate tax increases on top
earners have smaller behavioral effects than
very high rates would suggest.

lessons.title: Economic Development
lessons.content:
Economic development refers to the long-run process by which low-income countries improve living standards, productivity, and institutional quality. It is distinct from short-run economic growth (changes in GDP).

The sources of long-run economic growth include capital accumulation, human capital investment, technological progress, and institutional quality. Countries that invest in education, enforce property rights reliably, maintain rule of law, and have stable governments tend to grow faster.

The poverty trap is a concept in development economics describing situations where being poor makes it very hard to escape poverty. Without savings, a poor household cannot invest in education or capital. Without education or capital, income stays low. Without income, savings are impossible.

Microfinance is an approach that tries to break poverty traps by providing small loans to poor entrepreneurs who lack access to traditional banking. The empirical evidence on microfinance's impact on poverty is more mixed than early advocates hoped.

Foreign aid remains controversial among development economists. Some argue it provides critical resources for investment in poor countries. Others argue it can distort incentives, create dependency, and undermine local markets and governance.

Institutions are increasingly viewed as central to development. Countries with strong property rights, low corruption, and reliable legal systems grow faster. This explains why countries with similar natural resources can have vastly different growth trajectories.

examples.label: Sources of Long-Run Growth
examples.code:
Country grows from $1,000 per capita to $50,000 per capita
over a century. What drives this?

Capital accumulation:
Firms invest in more and better machines.
Each worker produces more with better tools.

Human capital:
Education rates rise. Workforce becomes more skilled.
Productivity per worker increases.

Technology:
Inventions and innovation raise the production frontier.
The same inputs produce more output.

Institutions:
Property rights are secure. Contracts are enforced.
Business investment is safer. Corruption is low.
People can capture the returns from their investments.

All four factors compound over decades into enormous growth.

questions.q: What does the Gini coefficient measure?
options: The average income of a country's population.
The total income of the wealthiest 10% of a population.
The degree of income inequality, from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (maximum inequality).
The poverty rate of a country.
questions.explanation: The Gini coefficient summarizes the entire income distribution in a single number. A country with Gini = 0 would have everyone earning exactly the same. A country with Gini = 1 would have all income going to one person.

questions.q: What is the efficiency-equity tradeoff in redistribution policy?
options: The tradeoff between economic growth and environmental protection.
The tension between reducing inequality through taxes and transfers and the potential disincentive effects on work and investment that high taxes create.
The choice between cash transfers and in-kind benefits.
The tradeoff between funding education versus healthcare.
questions.explanation: Redistribution can reduce inequality (equity) but may reduce incentives for the taxed to earn and the recipients to work (efficiency). The empirical question is how large these effects are at different levels of taxation and transfer.

questions.q: What is a poverty trap?
options: A government program that keeps people dependent on benefits.
A situation where being poor makes escaping poverty very difficult because poverty prevents the investments that would increase income.
A neighborhood with high concentrations of poverty.
An economic recession that disproportionately harms the poor.
questions.explanation: Poverty traps are self-reinforcing mechanisms. Without income, saving is impossible. Without savings, investment in education or capital is impossible. Without investment, income stays low. The cycle persists without outside intervention.

questions.q: Why are institutions considered central to long-run economic development?
options: Because government spending drives all growth.
Because countries with secure property rights, low corruption, and rule of law allow people to invest and capture returns from their efforts, fueling growth.
Because technology only develops in countries with strong governments.
Because trade only happens between countries with similar institutions.
questions.explanation: Secure property rights ensure that investment is not confiscated. Rule of law means contracts are enforced. Low corruption means businesses do not pay bribes rather than producing value. These institutional conditions make productive activity worthwhile and investment safe.

lessons.title: Protocol: Identify the Policy Goal
lessons.prompt:
A government introduces a policy where everyone below the poverty line receives monthly payments that decrease gradually as their income rises, fully phasing out once they earn 150% of the poverty line.

Is the primary goal of this policy efficiency, equity, or both?

Write: efficiency, equity, or both.

validation.message: error: the policy pursues equity by transferring income to low earners. it also pursues efficiency by gradually phasing out the benefit rather than cutting it abruptly, which would create a 100% marginal tax rate and eliminate the incentive to earn more. gradual phase-out preserves work incentives while still redistributing income.

lessons.title: The Economics of Climate Change
lessons.content:
Climate change is the largest externality problem in economic history. Burning fossil fuels imposes costs on current and future generations worldwide, but those costs are not reflected in the price of fuel. The result is that fossil fuels are overconsumed from society's perspective.

Carbon pricing is the primary market-based solution proposed by economists. A carbon tax puts a price on carbon emissions equal to the estimated social cost of carbon. This raises the private cost of burning fossil fuels to equal the social cost, reducing consumption to the optimal level and generating revenue the government can use or return to citizens.

A cap and trade system sets a total cap on carbon emissions and issues permits equal to that cap. Firms can trade permits. Firms that can reduce emissions cheaply sell their permits to firms that cannot. The total level of emissions is controlled, and reductions happen where they are cheapest, minimizing total cost.

The social cost of carbon is an estimate of the economic damages caused by emitting one additional ton of CO2. Estimates range from $50 to $200+ per ton depending on assumptions about discount rates (how much weight to give future damages), climate sensitivity, and economic impacts.

Discount rates are central to climate economics. Future damages must be discounted to present value for cost-benefit analysis. High discount rates make future damages seem small, reducing the urgency of action. Low discount rates make future damages loom large. This is as much a philosophical choice about our obligations to future generations as an economic one.

examples.label: Carbon Tax vs Cap and Trade
examples.code:
Both aim to reduce carbon emissions.

Carbon tax:
- Sets price of emissions (e.g., $50/ton CO2)
- Quantity of emissions is uncertain (depends on response)
- Revenue goes to government
- Predictable cost for businesses

Cap and trade:
- Sets quantity of emissions (total cap)
- Price of permits is uncertain (fluctuates)
- Revenue from permit auctions goes to government
- Guarantees meeting the emissions target

Both are more economically efficient than
command-and-control regulations that dictate
specific technologies or behaviors.

lessons.title: Automation and the Future of Work
lessons.content:
Technological change has always disrupted labor markets. The Industrial Revolution automated agricultural and artisan work. The computer revolution automated clerical and manufacturing work. Today, artificial intelligence and robotics are automating a widening range of cognitive tasks.

Economists generally believe that technological change, over the long run, creates more jobs than it destroys. New technologies increase productivity, which increases real incomes, which increases demand for goods and services, which creates demand for workers. The Luddites who smashed weaving machines in 19th century England were eventually proven wrong: employment in textiles grew after mechanization.

However, the short and medium run can involve significant disruption. Workers whose specific skills become obsolete face structural unemployment that requires retraining. The gains from automation tend to be concentrated among capital owners and high-skill workers who complement technology, while the losses are concentrated among routine-task workers.

The debate about AI is particularly intense because AI potentially automates cognitive tasks across many sectors simultaneously, affecting a broader range of workers than previous waves of automation. Whether this time is fundamentally different or follows historical patterns is genuinely uncertain.

Policy responses include investment in education and retraining, strengthening portable benefits that are not tied to specific employers, and potentially broader redistribution through the tax system to share the productivity gains from automation more widely.

examples.label: Historical Pattern of Automation
examples.code:
1900: 41% of US workforce in agriculture.
2020: 1.4% of US workforce in agriculture.

Huge productivity gains from mechanization.
Most farm jobs eliminated.
Yet total employment rose dramatically.

Where did the workers go?
Manufacturing, services, healthcare, education,
technology, entertainment, finance.
These sectors grew because agricultural
productivity gains raised incomes
and created demand for new products.

Historical lesson: technology destroys specific jobs
but creates broad economic growth that generates
demand for different types of work.

lessons.title: Housing Markets
lessons.content:
Housing is one of the most important markets in any economy, accounting for roughly a third of consumer spending when rent and related costs are included. It is also one of the most regulated, and the consequences of those regulations are widely debated.

Housing supply is highly inelastic in many cities due to land scarcity, zoning regulations, and long construction timelines. When demand for housing rises (because a city adds jobs), prices rise substantially if supply cannot keep pace.

Zoning laws determine what can be built where. Restrictions on multifamily housing, minimum lot sizes, height limits, and parking requirements all limit housing supply. Economists broadly agree that restrictive zoning in high-demand cities is a major cause of housing unaffordability and contributes to inequality by pricing workers out of high-productivity cities.

Rent control is a price ceiling applied to housing. As discussed earlier, it creates a shortage, reduces housing quality over time, and can freeze tenants in place because moving means losing below-market rent. It benefits current tenants at the expense of future renters and the overall housing stock.

Homelessness is a housing market failure at the extreme end. It results from a combination of extremely low incomes, mental illness, addiction, and insufficient low-income housing supply. Pure market outcomes, absent intervention, do not provide housing for those whose willingness to pay is below the cost of provision.

examples.label: Housing Supply and Price
examples.code:
City A (low regulation): New housing built freely.
Demand increases as tech sector grows.
Supply also increases: new apartments constructed.
Price rise is modest. Workers can afford to move in.

City B (high regulation): Strict zoning, limited construction.
Demand increases as tech sector grows.
Supply barely increases: few permits granted.
Prices spike dramatically.
Low and middle-income workers cannot afford to stay.
City segregates by income.

Same demand shock, opposite outcomes due to supply response.

lessons.title: Healthcare as an Economic Problem
lessons.content:
Healthcare markets fail in ways that distinguish them from ordinary goods markets. Understanding these failures explains why healthcare is so heavily regulated and why pure market provision of healthcare is considered inadequate by most economists.

Information asymmetry is severe. Patients do not know what treatments they need. They must rely on providers who both recommend and supply treatment, creating a principal-agent problem where the agent (doctor) has different incentives than the principal (patient).

Insurance creates moral hazard: insured patients consume more healthcare than they would if paying full price, increasing costs for everyone. Insurance markets also suffer from adverse selection.

Healthcare has significant positive externalities (vaccination prevents transmission to others) and is sometimes considered a right rather than just a good, which creates political pressure for universal access.

The US spends more than any other high-income country on healthcare as a share of GDP, yet achieves average or below-average health outcomes on many measures compared to peer countries. This is partly explained by high administrative costs, provider market concentration, and fee-for-service payment models that reward volume of treatment rather than health outcomes.

Universal healthcare systems (as in Canada, the UK, and most of Europe) address market failures through government provision or strict regulation. They achieve lower per-capita spending than the US system while covering entire populations. The tradeoffs involve potential wait times, limits on some treatments, and different innovation incentives.

examples.label: Market Failures in Healthcare
examples.code:
1. Information asymmetry:
Patient does not know if surgery is necessary.
Doctor recommends surgery and also performs it.
Conflict of interest embedded in the market.

2. Adverse selection:
Sick people want insurance. Healthy people opt out.
Insurance pool is skewed. Premiums rise.
More healthy people exit. Premiums rise further.

3. Moral hazard:
With insurance, a patient chooses the MRI
rather than monitoring. It costs them the same.
But it raises costs for all insureds.

4. Externalities:
Unvaccinated person can infect others.
Private decision, public health consequence.

questions.q: Why do economists generally prefer carbon pricing over command-and-control environmental regulation?
options: Carbon pricing raises more government revenue.
Carbon pricing allows emissions reductions to occur where they are cheapest, minimizing the total cost of achieving any given reduction target.
Command-and-control regulations always fail to reduce emissions.
Carbon pricing eliminates all emissions immediately.
questions.explanation: A price on carbon lets markets find the cheapest ways to reduce emissions. Firms that can cut emissions cheaply do so; those for whom it is costly pay the price and cut less. Total emissions fall at minimum cost. Command-and-control rules cannot achieve this efficiency.

questions.q: Why does restrictive zoning increase housing unaffordability?
options: Zoning increases construction costs by requiring higher quality materials.
Restrictive zoning limits the supply of housing in high-demand areas, so when demand rises, prices must rise instead of supply.
Zoning reduces property taxes, which makes housing more expensive.
Restrictive zoning increases the quality of housing, raising prices.
questions.explanation: Housing prices are determined by supply and demand. When zoning prevents new construction, supply cannot respond to rising demand. Prices absorb all the adjustment, often dramatically, pricing out low and middle-income residents.

questions.q: What is the principal-agent problem in healthcare?
options: When insurance companies deny claims to patients.
When patients cannot pay for necessary care.
When the agent (doctor) who recommends treatment also provides and profits from that treatment, creating potentially misaligned incentives.
When governments regulate drug prices.
questions.explanation: In healthcare, doctors are agents acting on behalf of patients (principals) who lack the knowledge to evaluate recommendations. When providers profit from the treatments they recommend, their incentives may not perfectly align with patient welfare.

questions.q: What does economic history suggest about the long-run employment effects of automation?
options: Automation permanently reduces total employment.
Automation always increases employment immediately.
Historically, automation has destroyed specific jobs but increased productivity and income, generating demand for new types of work, with net positive employment effects over the long run.
The effects of automation are random and unpredictable.
questions.explanation: Historical waves of automation (agricultural mechanization, factory machines, computers) destroyed specific job categories but did not cause permanent mass unemployment. Productivity gains raised incomes and demand, creating new employment in different sectors.

questions.q: What is a cap and trade system for carbon emissions?
options: A system where the government sets a maximum price for carbon.
A government program that subsidizes renewable energy.
A system that sets a total cap on emissions and allows firms to buy and sell permits, ensuring reductions happen where they are cheapest.
A regulatory system that bans the most polluting industries.
questions.explanation: Cap and trade controls total emissions by limiting permits. Trading allows efficiency: firms that can cut emissions cheaply sell permits to those for whom cutting is costly. The environmental goal is met at the lowest possible economic cost.

lessons.title: Protocol: Market Failure Identification
lessons.prompt:
A pharmaceutical company develops a new vaccine that prevents a highly contagious disease. Each person vaccinated not only protects themselves but also reduces the risk of infection for everyone around them.

What type of market failure does this represent, and in which direction will the market underprovide or overprovide the vaccine?

Write: positive externality, underprovide OR negative externality, overprovide.

validation.message: error: vaccination creates positive externalities: benefits flow to third parties not involved in the vaccination decision. because buyers do not capture all the benefits (some go to others), private willingness to pay is below the social benefit. the market will underprovide vaccination relative to the socially optimal level.
